bridge.title: InfoFlow KV: Information-Flow-Aware KV Recomputation for Long Context
bridge.abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for long-context question answering is bottlenecked by inference-time prefilling over large retrieved contexts. A common strategy is to precompute key-value (KV) caches for individual documents and selectively recompute a small subset of tokens to restore global causal dependencies, but existing methods rely on heuristics or representation discrepancies without modeling whether selected tokens can effectively influence generation. We cast selective KV recomputation as an information flow problem and show that a simple attention-norm signal from the query reliably identifies tokens that are both semantically relevant and structurally positioned to propagate information, when computed under an inference-consistent RoPE geometry. We therefore reconstruct global positional assignments for retrieved chunks and introduce an information-flow-guided chunk reordering strategy. Experiments on LLM and VLM benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains over prior methods under comparable efficiency budgets.
bridge.why_now: Long-context RAG is running into prefill latency and KV reuse limits right now, and this paper offers a concrete inference-time rule for what to recompute instead of relying on cruder heuristics. That makes it timely for teams trying to make retrieved context cheaper without giving up answer quality.
bridge.why_not_higher: It is still a serving optimization for a specific retrieved-context setup, not a broad recipe for all long-context inference. The abstract points to benchmark gains, but not yet to clear production-scale hardware or end-to-end deployment validation.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow to retrieved-context KV reuse
Benchmark evidence stronger than deployment evidence
Public interest is solid but not mass-audience level
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Show robust end-to-end wins in real RAG serving stacks across multiple model families and hardware targets under strict latency budgets.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if long-context RAG gets faster not by caching more, but by recomputing only the tokens that can actually carry information to the answer?

bridge.title: Beyond Scattered Acceptance: Fast and Coherent Inference for DLMs via Longest Stable Prefixes
bridge.abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) promise highly parallel text generation, yet their practical inference speed is often bottlenecked by suboptimal decoding schedulers. Standard approaches rely on 'scattered acceptance'-committing high confidence tokens at disjoint positions throughout the sequence. This approach inadvertently fractures the Key-Value (KV) cache, destroys memory locality, and forces the model into costly, repeated repairs across unstable token boundaries. To resolve this, we present the Longest Stable Prefix (LSP) scheduler, a training-free and model-agnostic inference paradigm based on monolithic prefix absorption. In each denoising step, LSP evaluates token stability via a single forward pass, dynamically identifies a contiguous left-aligned block of stable predictions, and snaps its boundary to natural linguistic or structural delimiters before an atomic commitment. This prefix-first topology yields dual benefits: systemically, it converts fragmented KV cache updates into efficient, contiguous appends; algorithmically, it preserves bidirectional lookahead over a geometrically shrinking active suffix, drastically reducing token flip rates and denoiser calls. Extensive evaluations on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B demonstrate that LSP accelerates inference by up to 3.4x across rigorous benchmarks including mathematical reasoning, code generation, multilingual (CJK) tasks, and creative writing while matching or slightly improving output quality. By fundamentally restructuring the commitment topology, LSP bridges the gap between the theoretical parallelism of DLMs and practical hardware efficiency.
bridge.why_now: Parallel text generation only matters if it survives contact with real inference systems, and this paper argues the scheduler itself has been the hidden bottleneck. The longest-stable-prefix idea turns a DLM decoding trick into a concrete KV-cache locality and serving-efficiency story, which makes it timely for both model and systems audiences.
bridge.why_not_higher: Diffusion language models are still less central than mainstream autoregressive LLM deployments, so the immediate audience is smaller than for a broadly reusable AR serving result. The abstract also shows strong task coverage on 7B-8B research models, but not yet clear production-engine or larger-scale deployment evidence.
downgrade_reasons: DLM adoption is still early relative to mainstream LLM serving
Evidence is centered on research-scale models rather than frontier deployments
Hardware and inference-stack details are lighter than the scheduler claim
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same locality and speedup gains on larger diffusion language models inside real serving stacks with clear wall-clock and hardware-efficiency measurements.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if diffusion language models have been slow not because parallel text generation is a dead end, but because their schedulers keep shattering the KV cache?

bridge.title: Why Smaller Is Slower? Dimensional Misalignment in Compressed LLMs
bridge.abstract: Post-training compression reduces LLM parameter counts but often produces irregular tensor dimensions that degrade GPU performance -- a phenomenon we call \emph{dimensional misalignment}. We present a full-stack analysis tracing root causes at three levels: framework, library, and hardware. The key insight is that model inference becomes slower because the resulting dimensions are unfriendly with the GPU execution stack. For example, compressing Llama-3-8B with activation-aware singular value decomposition (ASVD) has 15\% fewer parameters yet runs no faster than the uncompressed baseline, because 95\% of its dimensions are misaligned.   We propose \textbf{GAC} (GPU-Aligned Compression), a new compression paradigm that wraps any dimension-reducing compressor and re-selects hardware-aligned dimensions via multi-choice knapsack optimization under the same parameter budget. We evaluate GAC on Llama-3-8B with ASVD and LLM-Pruner, achieving 100\% alignment and recovering up to 1.5$\times$ speedup while preserving model quality.
bridge.why_now: Compression is a live serving strategy right now, and this paper shows that fewer parameters do not automatically translate into lower latency on current GPU stacks. It gives practitioners a concrete hardware-aware fix they can apply under the same parameter budget.
bridge.why_not_higher: The abstract-level evidence is still centered on Llama-3-8B and two compression methods, so the cross-model and cross-hardware generality is not fully established yet. The memory/storage link is adjacent rather than central; this is mainly an inference systems and hardware-alignment story.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is adjacent rather than core
Evaluation appears limited to one model family and a small set of compressors
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Broader validation across more model scales, GPU stacks, and compression pipelines would make this a stronger must-cover result.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if your compressed LLM got smaller on paper but stayed just as slow because its tensor shapes stopped matching how GPUs actually run?

bridge.title: Diagnosing FP4 inference: a layer-wise and block-wise sensitivity analysis of NVFP4 and MXFP4
bridge.abstract: Quantization addresses the high resource demand for large language models (LLMs) by alleviating memory pressure and bandwidth congestion and providing significantly scaled compute power with a tolerable impact on accuracy. Four-bit floating point (FP4), the lowest-precision format that preserves essential numerical properties such as exponent and sign, has begun to be adopted in cutting-edge architectures, including Blackwell and AMD CDNA, to support LLM quantization and reduce deployment costs. Although aggressive quantization can yield efficiency gains, the quantization sensitivity of within-transformer layers and whether these sensitivities generalize across existing FP4 formats and model scales remain underexplored. To elucidate quantization sensitivity, this study conducts a systematic analysis of two FP4 formats, MXFP4 and NVFP4, across three Qwen2.5 model scales (0.5B, 7B, and 14B), using controlled component-wise and block-wise isolation methodologies. We observe that MLP up- and down-projection layers consistently dominate in terms of sensitivity, while gate and attention projections are moderately and substantially less sensitive to FP4 quantization, respectively. We further find that sensitivity does not universally localize to the final blocks, but early blocks can be highly sensitive, particularly under MXFP4. Our results provide a diagnostic characterization of the inference behavior of FP4 across components, depths, and FP4 formats.
bridge.why_now: FP4 inference is becoming operationally relevant as vendors push Blackwell- and CDNA-era low-precision serving. A paper that maps which transformer components and depths actually break under NVFP4 and MXFP4 is timely for teams evaluating whether FP4 is usable beyond marketing throughput numbers.
bridge.why_not_higher: This reads more like a diagnostic study than a deployment recipe, and the abstract only demonstrates the effect on one model family across three scales. That makes it useful to inference and hardware practitioners, but not yet an obvious broad-audience episode.
downgrade_reasons: Moderate broad-audience appeal
Limited model-family diversity
No end-to-end serving outcome is highlighted
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same sensitivity pattern across more model families and connect it to a practical mixed-precision FP4 serving strategy with clear deployment gains.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: FP4 promises cheaper LLM inference, but which transformer layers can actually survive four-bit floating point before model quality starts to crack?

bridge.title: Network Design for Wafer-Scale Systems with Wafer-on-Wafer Hybrid Bonding
bridge.abstract: Transformer-based large language models are increasingly constrained by data movement as communication bandwidth drops sharply beyond the chip boundary. Wafer-scale integration using wafer-on-wafer hybrid bonding alleviates this limitation by providing ultra-high bandwidth between reticles on bonded wafers. In this paper, we investigate how the physical placement of reticles on wafers influences the achievable network topology and the resulting communication performance. Starting from a 2D mesh-like baseline, we propose four reticle placements (Aligned, Interleaved, Rotated, and Contoured) that improve throughput by up to 250%, reduce latency by up to 36%, and decrease energy per transmitted byte by up to 38%.
bridge.why_now: As LLM bottlenecks keep shifting from compute to data movement, wafer-scale packaging is becoming a serious systems lever. This paper argues that reticle placement is a first-order determinant of bandwidth, latency, and energy rather than a packaging footnote.
bridge.why_not_higher: The path from this result to what most AI teams can change in the next quarter is long, and the abstract does not show end-to-end gains on real training or serving stacks. It reads more like an architecture design study than an immediately actionable systems recipe.
downgrade_reasons: Immediate audience is mostly hardware and systems specialists
Evidence appears topology-level rather than full LLM deployment
Wafer-on-wafer scope is narrower than mainstream accelerator work
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Show end-to-end LLM training or inference speedups on realistic collective patterns or production-scale workloads, not just topology metrics.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: If AI is now limited by moving bytes instead of doing math, how much speed can you unlock just by rotating and rearranging reticles on a bonded wafer?

bridge.title: Parallelization Strategies for Dense LLM Deployment: Navigating Through Application-Specific Tradeoffs and Bottlenecks
bridge.abstract: Breakthroughs in the generative AI domain have fueled an explosion of large language model (LLM)-powered applications, whose workloads fundamentally consist of sequences of inferences through transformer architectures. Within this rapidly expanding ecosystem, dense LLMs--those that activate all model parameters for each token generation--form the foundation for advanced expert-based variants. Dense models continue to dominate because of their strong generalization ability, scalability, ease of fine-tuning, and versatility across diverse tasks. In LLM inference systems, performance is mainly characterized by latency, response time, and throughput (i.e., tokens generated per unit of time). Latency and throughput are inherently coupled: optimizing for one often comes at the expense of the other. Moreover, batching strategies and parallelism configurations, which are essential when dense model parameters exceed device memory capacity, can significantly affect both latency and overall system throughput. This paper (i) investigates the workloads of two representative dense LLMs--Llama-3.1-70B and Llama-3.1-405B, focusing in particular on intra-node parallelization schemes, (ii) analyzes how input characteristics, batching, and parallelism strategies influence latency flexibility and the latency-throughput tradeoff, and (iii) identifies key performance bottlenecks that inform design choices for meeting service-level agreements (SLAs) and sustaining inference quality. Our empirical evaluations reveal that Tensor Parallelism (TP) improves the latency objectives while Pipeline Parallelism (PP) is better-suited for throughput-oriented applications. We highlight that their hybrid usage by controlling the TP and PP degrees provides control over the latency-throughput interplay.
bridge.why_now: Serving 70B to 405B dense LLMs is now an operational bottleneck, and teams need clearer rules for trading off latency against throughput under real SLA pressure. This paper gives a timely, concrete comparison of TP, PP, and hybrid setups on representative large models.
bridge.why_not_higher: The contribution looks more like strong empirical characterization of known parallelism choices than a new serving method or systems primitive. The memory/storage angle is real but adjacent, centered on capacity and communication pressure rather than cache, offload, paging, or tiering mechanisms.
downgrade_reasons: Mostly a benchmark-and-tradeoff paper, not a new mechanism
Memory/storage connection is adjacent rather than core
Intra-node focus narrows how far the conclusions travel
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A clearer new policy or mechanism that materially shifts the latency-throughput frontier across multiple hardware settings would raise it.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: When a dense 70B or 405B model no longer fits cleanly on one box, should you split it with tensor parallelism or pipeline it across stages, and which bottleneck actually decides?

bridge.title: BASIS: Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars for "Ghost Backpropagation"
bridge.abstract: The activation memory required for exact backpropagation scales linearly with network depth, context length, and feature dimensionality, forming an O(L * BN ) spatial bottleneck (where B is the sequence-batch cardinality and N is the feature dimension). This constraint historically throttles the scaling of deep neural networks. While randomized automatic differentiation attempts to mitigate this, it historically suffers from catastrophic variance. In this paper, we introduce BASIS (Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars), an efficient backpropagation algorithm that fully decouples activation memory from the batch and sequence dimensions. BASIS propagates the exact error signal (dX) to preserve flawless gradient flow, but computes the weight updates (dW) using massively compressed rank-R tensors. To solve the foundational instability of sketched gradients, we propose two novel mechanisms: Balanced Hashing, which strictly eliminates off-diagonal collision variance, and Invariant Scalars, a principled bias-variance tradeoff that deterministically preserves the exact continuous energy norm of the spatial geometry. Theoretically, BASIS reduces activation memory to O(L * RN ) and heavily decreases the backward pass matrix-multiplication footprint. Empirically, training a GPT architecture for 50,000 steps validates our theoretical guarantees: at R = 32, BASIS achieves parity with (and marginally outperforms) exact backpropagation validation loss (6.575 vs. 6.616), acting as an implicit regularizer. Remarkably, the stabilized magnitude trajectory allows the model to converge smoothly even under extreme spatial compression (R = 1), proving the extreme robustness of the estimator. The code is available at https://github.com/VladimerKhasia/basis
bridge.why_now: Training-time activation memory is still a real scaling constraint, and this paper targets it directly rather than incidentally. A method that keeps exact error propagation while sketching weight-gradient state is relevant to current efforts to push deeper and longer-context LLM training under fixed memory budgets.
bridge.why_not_higher: The abstract only shows a 50,000-step GPT run and does not establish realistic scale, hardware efficiency, or robustness across model families. It also does not show whether BASIS beats stronger practical baselines such as recomputation, checkpointing, or other low-memory training methods on end-to-end cost.
downgrade_reasons: Single short-run GPT evaluation
No large-scale hardware or throughput evidence
Unclear advantage over existing memory-saving training baselines
No external validation or adoption signal
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Show replicated results on larger LLM training runs with wall-clock, throughput, and peak-memory comparisons against checkpointing and related low-memory backprop baselines.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you throw away most of backprop's activation state, keep exact gradient flow, and still train a GPT without the usual variance blow-up?

bridge.title: Balancing Coverage and Draft Latency in Vocabulary Trimming for Faster Speculative Decoding
bridge.abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates inference for Large Language Models by using a lightweight draft model to propose candidate tokens that are verified in parallel by a larger target model. Prior work shows that the draft model often dominates speculative decoding latency, since it generates tokens sequentially and incurs high cost from its language modeling head as vocabulary size grows. This exposes a fundamental trade-off in draft model design: larger vocabularies improve token coverage and agreement with the target model, but incur higher draft latency, while smaller vocabularies reduce latency at the risk of missing tokens required for accurate draft generation. We address this trade-off through vocabulary trimming for draft models, motivated by the observation that domain-specific workloads use only a small fraction of the full vocabulary. We cast draft vocabulary selection as a constrained optimization problem that balances token coverage and draft latency. Coverage is computed over assistant responses in the training data, while latency is estimated using architecture-aware FLOPs that capture the cost of the language modeling head as a function of vocabulary size. We optimize a utility function with a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator to efficiently explore the coverage-latency Pareto frontier under a minimum coverage constraint. Experiments show improved speculative decoding throughput while reducing draft vocabularies by up to 97% with high coverage. On domain-specific tasks, we achieve up to 16% latency reduction and 20% throughput improvement, and up to 6.7% throughput gains on diverse out-of-distribution tasks.
bridge.why_now: Speculative decoding is an active LLM serving optimization area, and this paper targets a real bottleneck people often underweight: draft-model vocabulary head cost. It is timely because many production deployments are specialized enough that trimming the draft vocabulary can improve latency and throughput without changing the target model.
bridge.why_not_higher: The strongest gains appear tied to domain-specific workloads rather than general-purpose LLM serving, so the audience is narrower than a top-tier inference paper. The memory angle is concrete but adjacent: this is mainly a draft-latency optimization, not a core KV-cache, paging, offload, or bandwidth-scheduling paper.
downgrade_reasons: Largest gains depend on domain-specific token distributions
Memory/storage connection is adjacent rather than core
Abstract does not establish broad production-scale validation across common serving stacks
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Show consistent end-to-end gains on mainstream open LLM serving setups and larger general-purpose models, with clearer evidence that vocabulary trimming pays off outside specialized domains.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the hidden bottleneck in speculative decoding is not the big model, but the draft model's giant vocabulary head, and shrinking it by 97% still keeps the tokens that matter?

bridge.title: POET-X: Memory-efficient LLM Training by Scaling Orthogonal Transformation
bridge.abstract: Efficient and stable training of large language models (LLMs) remains a core challenge in modern machine learning systems. To address this challenge, Reparameterized Orthogonal Equivalence Training (POET), a spectrum-preserving framework that optimizes each weight matrix through orthogonal equivalence transformation, has been proposed. Although POET provides strong training stability, its original implementation incurs high memory consumption and computational overhead due to intensive matrix multiplications. To overcome these limitations, we introduce POET-X, a scalable and memory-efficient variant that performs orthogonal equivalence transformations with significantly reduced computational cost. POET-X maintains the generalization and stability benefits of POET while achieving substantial improvements in throughput and memory efficiency. In our experiments, POET-X enables the pretraining of billion-parameter LLMs on a single Nvidia H100 GPU, and in contrast, standard optimizers such as AdamW run out of memory under the same settings.
bridge.why_now: Training efficiency is a live concern, and the claim that billion-parameter LLM pretraining fits on a single H100 gives this paper a timely, concrete hook. If the method really preserves stability while cutting memory cost, it could matter for resource-constrained model builders.
bridge.why_not_higher: This is still an optimizer/reparameterization story, not a core memory-systems paper about cache, offload, tiering, or bandwidth management. It also does not yet read as a clearly drop-in, broadly adopted training recipe from the abstract alone.
downgrade_reasons: Memory angle is adjacent rather than memory-systems core
Adoption depends on a specialized POET-style training framework
Breadth of validation across models and scales is still unclear
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Clear head-to-head wins on mainstream LLM pretraining stacks across multiple scales, with strong final-quality and wall-clock results in addition to memory savings, would raise priority.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an orthogonal reparameterization make billion-parameter LLM training fit on one H100 without sacrificing stability or quality?

bridge.title: AI+HW 2035: Shaping the Next Decade
bridge.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) and hardware (HW) are advancing at unprecedented rates, yet their trajectories have become inseparably intertwined. The global research community lacks a cohesive, long-term vision to strategically coordinate the development of AI and HW. This fragmentation constrains progress toward holistic, sustainable, and adaptive AI systems capable of learning, reasoning, and operating efficiently across cloud, edge, and physical environments. The future of AI depends not only on scaling intelligence, but on scaling efficiency, achieving exponential gains in intelligence per joule, rather than unbounded compute consumption. Addressing this grand challenge requires rethinking the entire computing stack. This vision paper lays out a 10-year roadmap for AI+HW co-design and co-development, spanning algorithms, architectures, systems, and sustainability. We articulate key insights that redefine scaling around energy efficiency, system-level integration, and cross-layer optimization. We identify key challenges and opportunities, candidly assess potential obstacles and pitfalls, and propose integrated solutions grounded in algorithmic innovation, hardware advances, and software abstraction. Looking ahead, we define what success means in 10 years: achieving a 1000x improvement in efficiency for AI training and inference; enabling energy-aware, self-optimizing systems that seamlessly span cloud, edge, and physical AI; democratizing access to advanced AI infrastructure; and embedding human-centric principles into the design of intelligent systems. Finally, we outline concrete action items for academia, industry, government, and the broader community, calling for coordinated national initiatives, shared infrastructure, workforce development, cross-agency collaboration, and sustained public-private partnerships to ensure that AI+HW co-design becomes a unifying long-term mission.
bridge.why_now: As power, cost, and deployment constraints become central to AI progress, a cross-layer AI+HW roadmap from prominent authors is timely and gives listeners a strong frame for where efficiency work may head next.
bridge.why_not_higher: This is mainly a vision and coordination paper, not a demonstrated systems result on representative modern workloads. The memory/storage angle is adjacent rather than a concrete KV-cache, offload, paging, or bandwidth-reduction mechanism.
downgrade_reasons: Vision paper with limited empirical validation
Memory/storage link is adjacent, not core
Claims are aspirational and not tied to concrete deployment results
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A companion paper showing measured cross-layer gains on real LLM training or serving stacks would make the roadmap much more actionable.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: If the real bottleneck for the next decade of AI is not model ideas but joules, data movement, and hardware-software co-design, what would it actually take to get 1000x more intelligence per watt?

public.title: The Spike, the Sparse and the Sink: Anatomy of Massive Activations and Attention Sinks
public.abstract: We study two recurring phenomena in Transformer language models: massive activations, in which a small number of tokens exhibit extreme outliers in a few channels, and attention sinks, in which certain tokens attract disproportionate attention mass regardless of semantic relevance. Prior work observes that these phenomena frequently co-occur and often involve the same tokens, but their functional roles and causal relationship remain unclear. Through systematic experiments, we show that the co-occurrence is largely an architectural artifact of modern Transformer design, and that the two phenomena serve related but distinct functions. Massive activations operate globally: they induce near-constant hidden representations that persist across layers, effectively functioning as implicit parameters of the model. Attention sinks operate locally: they modulate attention outputs across heads and bias individual heads toward short-range dependencies. We identify the pre-norm configuration as the key choice that enables the co-occurrence, and show that ablating it causes the two phenomena to decouple.
public.why_now: Attention sinks and activation outliers keep resurfacing in LLM discussions, especially around long-context behavior and transformer internals. A paper that causally separates the two and ties their co-occurrence to pre-norm design is timely and easy to explain to a broad AI audience.
public.why_not_higher: It is more explanatory than operational: the paper sharpens our mental model of transformers, but it does not yet deliver a concrete training, serving, or evaluation recipe that builders will immediately adopt. The memory/storage angle is weak and should not be oversold.
downgrade_reasons: No direct KV-cache, offload, paging, or bandwidth mechanism
Limited immediate systems guidance for training or serving changes
public.what_would_raise_priority: Show that these findings translate into concrete architecture or serving changes on frontier LLMs with measurable long-context or cache-related gains.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do a few seemingly irrelevant tokens behave like hidden parameters inside transformers, and what does that reveal about the real function of attention sinks?

public.title: Reasoning Models Struggle to Control their Chains of Thought
public.abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring is a promising tool for detecting misbehaviors and understanding the motivations of modern reasoning models. However, if models can control what they verbalize in their CoT, it could undermine CoT monitorability. To measure this undesirable capability -- CoT controllability -- we introduce the CoT-Control evaluation suite, which includes tasks that require models to solve problems while adhering to CoT instructions, e.g., reasoning about a genetics question without using the word 'chromosome'. We show that reasoning models possess significantly lower CoT controllability than output controllability; for instance, Claude Sonnet 4.5 can control its CoT only 2.7% of the time but 61.9% when controlling its final output. We also find that CoT controllability is higher for larger models and decreases with more RL training, test-time compute, and increased problem difficulty. CoT controllability failures extend even to situations in which models are given incentives (as opposed to direct requests) to evade CoT monitors, although models exhibit slightly higher controllability when they are told they are being monitored. Similarly, eliciting controllability by adversarially optimizing prompts does not meaningfully increase controllability. Our results leave us cautiously optimistic that CoT controllability is currently unlikely to be a failure mode of CoT monitorability. However, the mechanism behind low controllability is not well understood. Given its importance for maintaining CoT monitorability, we recommend that frontier labs track CoT controllability in future models.
public.why_now: Reasoning-model safety and monitorability are live issues right now, and this paper asks a concrete version of a question many people have been hand-waving: can models strategically sanitize their own chain of thought. The result is timely because it is both surprising and cautiously reassuring for current CoT-monitoring practice.
public.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is absent, and the paper is still primarily an evaluation result rather than a method that directly changes training or serving. It also leaves the underlying mechanism unexplained, which limits how actionable the finding is.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily an eval benchmark rather than a deployment method
Mechanism behind low CoT controllability remains unclear
public.what_would_raise_priority: Clear evidence that this benchmark predicts real monitor-evasion risk in deployed frontier models, plus concrete guidance for training or evaluating systems against it, would raise priority.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Are reasoning models actually capable of hiding their motives, or are they still surprisingly bad at controlling the very chains of thought we hope to monitor?

public.title: KARL: Knowledge Agents via Reinforcement Learning
public.abstract: We present a system for training enterprise search agents via reinforcement learning that achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse suite of hard-to-verify agentic search tasks. Our work makes four core contributions. First, we introduce KARLBench, a multi-capability evaluation suite spanning six distinct search regimes, including constraint-driven entity search, cross-document report synthesis, tabular numerical reasoning, exhaustive entity retrieval, procedural reasoning over technical documentation, and fact aggregation over internal enterprise notes. Second, we show that models trained across heterogeneous search behaviors generalize substantially better than those optimized for any single benchmark. Third, we develop an agentic synthesis pipeline that employs long-horizon reasoning and tool use to generate diverse, grounded, and high-quality training data, with iterative bootstrapping from increasingly capable models. Fourth, we propose a new post-training paradigm based on iterative large-batch off-policy RL that is sample efficient, robust to train-inference engine discrepancies, and naturally extends to multi-task training with out-of-distribution generalization. Compared to Claude 4.6 and GPT 5.2, KARL is Pareto-optimal on KARLBench across cost-quality and latency-quality trade-offs, including tasks that were out-of-distribution during training. With sufficient test-time compute, it surpasses the strongest closed models. These results show that tailored synthetic data in combination with multi-task reinforcement learning enables cost-efficient and high-performing knowledge agents for grounded reasoning.
public.why_now: Agentic search and grounded knowledge work are active deployment fronts, and this paper claims a concrete post-training recipe that beats frontier closed models on cost, latency, and quality tradeoffs. The mix of synthetic data generation, multi-task RL, and out-of-distribution evaluation makes it timely beyond a single leaderboard bump.
public.why_not_higher: The strongest evidence lives on a custom enterprise-search suite, so the jump from knowledge agents to general-purpose agents is still not fully settled. It also has no real memory or storage mechanism, so it only lands on the public-interest lens.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage contribution
Core evidence depends on a custom benchmark and synthetic-data pipeline
Transfer beyond enterprise search agents is promising but not yet proven
public.what_would_raise_priority: Independent replication on open agentic benchmarks or clear transfer to broader tool-using assistants would raise it.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the next leap in enterprise AI is not a bigger model, but a search agent post-trained with synthetic data and RL to beat frontier assistants on grounded knowledge work?

public.title: Building Effective AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
public.abstract: The landscape of AI coding assistance is undergoing a fundamental shift from complex IDE plugins to versatile, terminal-native agents. Operating directly where developers manage source control, execute builds, and deploy environments, CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks. In this paper, we present OPENDEV, an open-source, command-line coding agent written in Rust, engineered specifically for this new paradigm. Effective autonomous assistance requires strict safety controls and highly efficient context management to prevent context bloat and reasoning degradation. OPENDEV overcomes these challenges through a compound AI system architecture with workload-specialized model routing, a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, lazy tool discovery, and adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations. Furthermore, it employs an automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions and counteracts instruction fade-out through event-driven system reminders. By enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance, offering a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.
public.why_now: Terminal-native coding agents are one of the clearest near-term AI product shifts, and this paper tries to turn scattered practice into an explicit architecture. The context-management and tool-orchestration lessons are timely for teams building long-horizon agents right now.
public.why_not_higher: The abstract reads more like a system design blueprint and experience report than a strongly validated research advance, with little evidence here about realistic benchmarks or hard comparisons. Its "memory" story is agent memory and context management, not memory or storage systems in the podcast's core sense.
downgrade_reasons: weak direct memory/storage relevance
evaluation realism unclear from abstract
single-agent-system blueprint more than a field-shifting result
public.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger empirical evidence on representative coding tasks, clear ablations for the scaffolding choices, and demonstrated wins versus current coding-agent baselines would raise it.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why are serious AI coding agents moving out of the IDE and into the terminal, and what scaffolding actually keeps them useful instead of drowning in their own context?

public.title: Leveraging LLM Parametric Knowledge for Fact Checking without Retrieval
public.abstract: Trustworthiness is a core research challenge for agentic AI systems built on Large Language Models (LLMs). To enhance trust, natural language claims from diverse sources, including human-written text, web content, and model outputs, are commonly checked for factuality by retrieving external knowledge and using an LLM to verify the faithfulness of claims to the retrieved evidence. As a result, such methods are constrained by retrieval errors and external data availability, while leaving the models intrinsic fact-verification capabilities largely unused. We propose the task of fact-checking without retrieval, focusing on the verification of arbitrary natural language claims, independent of their source. To study this setting, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework focused on generalization, testing robustness to (i) long-tail knowledge, (ii) variation in claim sources, (iii) multilinguality, and (iv) long-form generation. Across 9 datasets, 18 methods and 3 models, our experiments indicate that logit-based approaches often underperform compared to those that leverage internal model representations. Building on this finding, we introduce INTRA, a method that exploits interactions between internal representations and achieves state-of-the-art performance with strong generalization. More broadly, our work establishes fact-checking without retrieval as a promising research direction that can complement retrieval-based frameworks, improve scalability, and enable the use of such systems as reward signals during training or as components integrated into the generation process.
public.why_now: Trustworthy agentic AI is a live problem, and this paper argues that models' own internal knowledge can be used for factual verification instead of always depending on brittle retrieval. That is timely because many current pipelines over-index on external lookup while underusing the verifier already inside the model.
public.why_not_higher: This is still primarily a fact-checking method paper, not a clear change to how most teams build or serve modern AI systems today. The memory/storage lens is absent, and the abstract does not yet show decisive wins inside real production RAG or agent stacks.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Task-specific fact-checking framing limits breadth
No clear production-system integration evidence from the abstract
public.what_would_raise_priority: Show that INTRA materially improves factuality in deployed agent or RAG workflows on frontier models with competitive latency and cost against retrieval-heavy baselines.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Do LLMs already know enough to fact-check us from the inside, and if so, why are we still sending every doubtful claim out to retrieval first?

public.title: Judge Reliability Harness: Stress Testing the Reliability of LLM Judges
public.abstract: We present the Judge Reliability Harness, an open source library for constructing validation suites that test the reliability of LLM judges. As LLM based scoring is widely deployed in AI benchmarks, more tooling is needed to efficiently assess the reliability of these methods. Given a benchmark dataset and an LLM judge configuration, the harness generates reliability tests that evaluate both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for free-response and agentic task formats. We evaluate four state-of-the-art judges across four benchmarks spanning safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic behavior, and find meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types, highlighting opportunities to improve the robustness of LLM judges. No judge that we evaluated is uniformly reliable across benchmarks using our harness. For example, our preliminary experiments on judges revealed consistency issues as measured by accuracy in judging another LLM's ability to complete a task due to simple text formatting changes, paraphrasing, changes in verbosity, and flipping the ground truth label in LLM-produced responses. The code for this tool is available at: https://github.com/RANDCorporation/judge-reliability-harness
public.why_now: LLM judges are becoming default infrastructure for evals, benchmarks, and agent testing, so evidence that they are brittle to simple perturbations matters right now. A reusable harness for stress-testing judges fits a live pain point as more teams automate evaluation with model graders.
public.why_not_higher: This is mainly an evaluation harness plus benchmark findings, not a paper that changes model training or serving practice by itself. The evidence is useful but still limited in scope, and the abstract reads more like a warning shot than a decisive field-shifting result.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage angle
Primarily eval tooling rather than a build/serve breakthrough
Benchmark-heavy paper with limited systems depth
public.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that the harness changes real deployment decisions or exposes widespread failures in judges used by major public benchmarks would raise it.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the AI systems grading today's models are themselves unreliable, and a few trivial wording changes can flip the verdict?

public.title: Tool-Genesis: A Task-Driven Tool Creation Benchmark for Self-Evolving Language Agent
public.abstract: Research on self-evolving language agents has accelerated, drawing increasing attention to their ability to create, adapt, and maintain tools from task requirements. However, existing benchmarks predominantly rely on predefined specifications, which limits scalability and hinders truly autonomous evolution. While recent studies attempt to dynamically generate tools, they primarily emphasize downstream performance, resulting in a "black-box" evaluation that makes it difficult to attribute failures to specific causes. To address this, we propose Tool-Genesis, a diagnostic benchmark designed to quantify agent capabilities across multiple dimensions, including interface compliance, functional correctness, and downstream utility. Tool-Genesis evaluates whether agents can construct task-relevant tools solely from abstract requirements (without preset specifications) and use them to solve realistic problems. Crucially, we find that even state-of-the-art models struggle to produce precise tool interfaces or executable logic in a one-shot setting. These minor initial flaws are amplified through the pipeline, leading to a sharp degradation in downstream metrics. We hope Tool-Genesis will guide future research toward training and steering models to synthesize persistent, general-purpose tools that better address real-world challenges.
public.why_now: Agentic tool use is an active topic, and this benchmark targets a real gap: whether models can create usable tools from abstract requirements instead of relying on hand-written specs. Its core finding that small interface and logic errors cascade into major downstream failures is timely for teams building autonomous agents.
public.why_not_higher: This is primarily a benchmark paper, not a clear new method that changes how people build or serve AI systems today. It also has no direct memory or storage mechanism, so it does not advance the podcast's second editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Benchmark-heavy with limited direct deployment guidance
Abstract does not show strong real-world validation beyond evaluation tasks
public.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing specific training or agent-design changes that reliably improve tool creation on real developer or enterprise workflows would raise it.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What breaks when you stop giving AI agents prebuilt APIs and force them to invent their own tools from scratch?

public.title: Ensembling Language Models with Sequential Monte Carlo
public.abstract: Practitioners have access to an abundance of language models and prompting strategies for solving many language modeling tasks; yet prior work shows that modeling performance is highly sensitive to both choices. Classical machine learning ensembling techniques offer a principled approach: aggregate predictions from multiple sources to achieve better performance than any single one. However, applying ensembling to language models during decoding is challenging: naively aggregating next-token probabilities yields samples from a locally normalized, biased approximation of the generally intractable ensemble distribution over strings. In this work, we introduce a unified framework for composing $K$ language models into $f$-ensemble distributions for a wide range of functions $f\colon\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{K}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$. To sample from these distributions, we propose a byte-level sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) algorithm that operates in a shared character space, enabling ensembles of models with mismatching vocabularies and consistent sampling in the limit. We evaluate a family of $f$-ensembles across prompt and model combinations for various structured text generation tasks, highlighting the benefits of alternative aggregation strategies over traditional probability averaging, and showing that better posterior approximations can yield better ensemble performance.
public.why_now: Teams are actively mixing models and prompting strategies, and this paper gives a more principled way to ensemble them at decoding time instead of relying on ad hoc probability averaging. The byte-level SMC angle is timely because heterogeneous model stacks and structured generation workflows are becoming more common.
public.why_not_higher: The practical impact is still uncertain because the method looks inference-heavy and the abstract emphasizes structured text generation rather than mainstream serving workloads. It is also not a memory/storage paper in any direct sense, so it only competes on the public-interest lens.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Unclear deployment path for production inference stacks
Evidence appears task-focused rather than systems-realistic
May be too methodological for a top-tier episode this cycle
public.what_would_raise_priority: A clear result showing robust gains on widely used LLM workloads with acceptable inference cost would raise it.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if combining language models the obvious way is mathematically wrong, and the real fix looks more like particle filtering than prompt engineering?

public.title: RealWonder: Real-Time Physical Action-Conditioned Video Generation
public.abstract: Current video generation models cannot simulate physical consequences of 3D actions like forces and robotic manipulations, as they lack structural understanding of how actions affect 3D scenes. We present RealWonder, the first real-time system for action-conditioned video generation from a single image. Our key insight is using physics simulation as an intermediate bridge: instead of directly encoding continuous actions, we translate them through physics simulation into visual representations (optical flow and RGB) that video models can process. RealWonder integrates three components: 3D reconstruction from single images, physics simulation, and a distilled video generator requiring only 4 diffusion steps. Our system achieves 13.2 FPS at 480x832 resolution, enabling interactive exploration of forces, robot actions, and camera controls on rigid objects, deformable bodies, fluids, and granular materials. We envision RealWonder opens new opportunities to apply video models in immersive experiences, AR/VR, and robot learning. Our code and model weights are publicly available in our project website: https://liuwei283.github.io/RealWonder/
public.why_now: Physics-aware, interactive video generation is a live frontier for world models, robotics demos, and immersive AI interfaces, and this paper pairs that story with real-time performance. The physics-simulation bridge is also a clean, teachable idea for why action-conditioned video has been hard.
public.why_not_higher: This looks more like a strong vision and graphics application than a result that changes mainstream AI training or serving practice. Its memory and storage relevance is absent, and the path from this demo to broadly adopted AI workflows is still speculative.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited impact on mainstream AI system design
Narrow task focus despite strong demo appeal
public.what_would_raise_priority: A clearer demonstration that the physics-bridge method improves general world models, robot learning pipelines, or reusable simulation-to-video tooling beyond this specific task would raise priority.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the missing ingredient for controllable video generation is not a bigger model, but a real-time physics simulator that turns a single image into a world you can push, grab, and reshape?

public.title: Safer Reasoning Traces: Measuring and Mitigating Chain-of-Thought Leakage in LLMs
public.abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting improves LLM reasoning but can increase privacy risk by resurfacing personally identifiable information (PII) from the prompt into reasoning traces and outputs, even under policies that instruct the model not to restate PII. We study such direct, inference-time PII leakage using a model-agnostic framework that (i) defines leakage as risk-weighted, token-level events across 11 PII types, (ii) traces leakage curves as a function of the allowed CoT budget, and (iii) compares open- and closed-source model families on a structured PII dataset with a hierarchical risk taxonomy. We find that CoT consistently elevates leakage, especially for high-risk categories, and that leakage is strongly family- and budget-dependent. Increasing the reasoning budget can either amplify or attenuate leakage depending on the base model. We then benchmark lightweight inference-time gatekeepers: a rule-based detector, a TF-IDF + logistic regression classifier, a GLiNER-based NER model, and an LLM-as-judge, using risk-weighted F1, Macro-F1, and recall. No single method dominates across models or budgets, motivating hybrid, style-adaptive gatekeeping policies that balance utility and risk under a common, reproducible protocol.
public.why_now: Reasoning models are spreading quickly, and this paper isolates a concrete deployment risk: chain-of-thought can leak prompt PII even when the model is told not to repeat it. The budget-dependent leakage curves also make this timely for teams tuning reasoning depth rather than treating privacy as a fixed property.
public.why_not_higher: This is mainly an evaluation and gatekeeping paper, not a clear step-change in how models are built or served. The mitigation story is useful but still benchmark-heavy, with limited proof of low-friction deployment on messy real-world traffic.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily a benchmark and filtering study
Real-world deployment impact depends on whether CoT traces are exposed or retained
public.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that a lightweight mitigation preserves reasoning quality while reliably reducing leakage on production-like workloads would raise it to Cover now.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Do reasoning traces make LLMs smarter by the same mechanism that lets them quietly leak the secrets they were asked to protect?

memory.title: CRISP: Compressed Reasoning via Iterative Self-Policy Distillation
memory.abstract: Reasoning models think out loud, but much of what they say is noise. We introduce CRISP (Compressed Reasoning via Iterative Self-Policy Distillation), a method that teaches models to reason more concisely by distilling their own concise behavior back into themselves. The entire approach reduces to one idea: condition the same model on a ''be concise'' instruction to obtain teacher logits, and minimize per-token reverse KL on the student's own rollouts. No ground-truth answers, no token budgets, no difficulty estimators. Just self-distillation. Yet this simplicity belies surprising sophistication: CRISP automatically compresses easy problems aggressively while preserving the deliberation needed for hard ones. On Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-14B, we achieve 57--59% token reduction on MATH-500 while improving accuracy by 9--16 points absolute. On AIME 2024, the 14B model gains 10 points with 41% compression. Ablations show that qualitative conciseness instructions outperform explicit token targets, and periodic teacher refreshes yield a broad stable regime. The method generalizes across model families -- DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B improves accuracy by up to 5 points with 17--32% compression -- and transfers beyond math to multi-step agentic planning (DeepPlanning), reducing token usage by 42--51% while preserving planning quality. Code is available at https://github.com/HJSang/OPSD_Reasoning_Compression.
memory.why_now: Reasoning models are expensive partly because they overproduce chains of thought, so a method that cuts tokens roughly in half while improving accuracy lands directly on a live industry pain point. The paper is also unusually simple to explain and implement, which makes it more likely to influence current post-training practice quickly.
memory.why_not_higher: The memory/storage angle is indirect: shorter traces help KV cache footprint and serving cost, but the paper is not actually about cache management, offload, or bandwidth-aware systems design. The evidence is benchmark-strong but still lacks end-to-end serving data such as latency, throughput, and real deployment cost curves.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is adjacent rather than core
No direct KV-cache, bandwidth, latency, or throughput measurements
Evaluation is still centered on reasoning benchmarks plus a limited planning transfer test
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Real serving results showing consistent latency, throughput, and KV-cache savings on broader agentic or production reasoning workloads would raise priority.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the way to make reasoning models both smarter and cheaper is to teach them to think less out loud?

memory.title: SlideSparse: Fast and Flexible (2N-2):2N Structured Sparsity
memory.abstract: NVIDIA's 2:4 Sparse Tensor Cores deliver 2x throughput but demand strict 50% pruning -- a ratio that collapses LLM reasoning accuracy (Qwen3: 54% to 15%). Milder $(2N-2):2N$ patterns (e.g., 6:8, 25% pruning) preserve accuracy yet receive no hardware support, falling back to dense execution without any benefit from sparsity. We present SlideSparse, the first system to unlock Sparse Tensor Core acceleration for the $(2N-2):2N$ model family on commodity GPUs. Our Sliding Window Decomposition reconstructs any $(2N-2):2N$ weight block into $N-1$ overlapping 2:4-compliant windows without any accuracy loss; Activation Lifting fuses the corresponding activation rearrangement into per-token quantization at near-zero cost. Integrated into vLLM, SlideSparse is evaluated across various GPUs (A100, H100, B200, RTX 4090, RTX 5080, DGX-spark), precisions (FP4, INT8, FP8, BF16, FP16), and model families (Llama, Qwen, BitNet). On compute-bound workloads, the measured speedup ratio (1.33x) approaches the theoretical upper-bound $N/(N-1)=4/3$ at 6:8 weight sparsity in Qwen2.5-7B, establishing $(2N-2):2N$ as a practical path to accuracy-preserving LLM acceleration. Code available at https://github.com/bcacdwk/vllmbench.
memory.why_now: Serving teams want practical throughput gains on installed NVIDIA GPUs, but strict 2:4 sparsity has been too accuracy-destructive for modern LLMs. SlideSparse makes a milder sparsity regime usable inside a real serving stack on commodity hardware, which gives it immediate systems relevance.
memory.why_not_higher: The core story is sparse compute acceleration, not first-class memory or cache behavior, so it only partially fits the storage lens. The upside is also bounded and looks strongest in compute-bound settings, which limits how universal the headline win will be.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is adjacent rather than core
Best gains are shown in compute-bound workloads
Impact depends on broader adoption of sparse-pruned model checkpoints
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence of stable end-to-end latency and cost wins on widely deployed larger models in mainstream vLLM serving would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the sparsity pattern that actually preserves LLM accuracy could finally unlock NVIDIA sparse tensor cores and turn a mostly unusable hardware feature into a real serving win?

memory.title: Frequency-Aware Error-Bounded Caching for Accelerating Diffusion Transformers
memory.abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have emerged as the dominant architecture for high-quality image and video generation, yet their iterative denoising process incurs substantial computational cost during inference. Existing caching methods accelerate DiTs by reusing intermediate computations across timesteps, but they share a common limitation: treating the denoising process as uniform across time,depth, and feature dimensions. In this work, we identify three orthogonal axes of non-uniformity in DiT denoising: (1) temporal -- sensitivity to caching errors varies dramatically across the denoising trajectory; (2) depth -- consecutive caching decisions lead to cascading approximation errors; and (3) feature -- different components of the hidden state exhibit heterogeneous temporal dynamics. Based on these observations, we propose SpectralCache, a unified caching framework comprising Timestep-Aware Dynamic Scheduling (TADS), Cumulative Error Budgets (CEB), and Frequency-Decomposed Caching (FDC). On FLUX.1-schnell at 512x512 resolution, SpectralCache achieves 2.46x speedup with LPIPS 0.217 and SSIM 0.727, outperforming TeaCache (2.12x, LPIPS 0.215, SSIM 0.734) by 16% in speed while maintaining comparable quality (LPIPS difference < 1%). Our approach is training-free, plug-and-play, and compatible with existing DiT architectures.
memory.why_now: DiT inference cost is becoming a real product constraint for image and video generation, and a training-free 2.46x speedup on a recognizable model is operationally relevant. The paper also has a clear explanatory angle around non-uniformity across timesteps, layers, and frequency bands rather than another generic cache heuristic.
memory.why_not_higher: The scope is still narrow: this is an inference optimization for diffusion transformers, not a result that obviously changes mainstream AI serving beyond image generation. The memory angle is adjacent rather than core, and the abstract suggests limited breadth across models, resolutions, and hardware.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow to diffusion transformer inference rather than general AI systems
Memory/storage relevance is adjacent, not first-class
Evaluation breadth appears limited in the abstract
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Broader results across multiple DiT families, video workloads, and hardware settings, plus clearer latency and memory tradeoff data in realistic serving setups, would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why does diffusion waste so much work at the wrong timesteps, and can a frequency-aware cache cut nearly half the cost without visibly breaking the image?

memory.title: Scaling Agentic Capabilities, Not Context: Efficient Reinforcement Finetuning for Large Toolspaces
memory.abstract: Agentic systems operating over large tool ecosystems must plan and execute long-horizon workflows under weak or non-verifiable supervision. While frontier models mitigate these challenges through scale and large context budgets, small language models (SLMs) remain brittle: eager tool loading saturates context, execution errors compound over time, and sparse rewards limit learning. We introduce ATLAS, a reinforcement finetuning framework that enables SLMs to operate effectively in large-scale toolspace environments by learning how to acquire context and how to execute actions. Our approach makes two key contributions. First, we treat context control and execution structure as learnable decisions, combining iterative tool loading with programmatic tool orchestration to bound context growth and stabilize long-horizon trajectories. Second, we propose rubric-based reinforcement finetuning, which decomposes task success into structured, task-aligned criteria and enables scalable training using small judge models. Across MCP benchmarks, these design choices yield large and consistent gains over generic RL baselines, allowing a 4B SLM to approach frontier-agent performance under far tighter parameter and context budgets.
memory.why_now: Tool-using agents and MCP-style ecosystems are moving into the mainstream, and this paper targets a real bottleneck: getting smaller models to act competently without huge context budgets. The combination of selective tool loading and rubric-based RL is timely because teams are actively looking for cheaper agent stacks that still work over large toolspaces.
memory.why_not_higher: The memory angle is adjacent rather than core: this is mostly an agent-training and evaluation paper, not a paper about cache, paging, offload, or storage systems. The evidence also appears benchmark-centric, so it is not yet clear how well the gains survive messy production tool environments.
downgrade_reasons: Memory connection is indirect
Benchmark-heavy evidence
Agentic RL/tool-use space is crowded
Unclear real-world deployment validation
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger priority case would come from convincing production-style results showing robust gains on real tool ecosystems with explicit cost, latency, and context-budget tradeoffs.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if small models stop losing the agent race not by getting more context, but by learning when to fetch tools, how to structure actions, and how to train on richer rewards?

memory.title: Towards Multimodal Lifelong Understanding: A Dataset and Agentic Baseline
memory.abstract: While datasets for video understanding have scaled to hour-long durations, they typically consist of densely concatenated clips that differ from natural, unscripted daily life. To bridge this gap, we introduce MM-Lifelong, a dataset designed for Multimodal Lifelong Understanding. Comprising 181.1 hours of footage, it is structured across Day, Week, and Month scales to capture varying temporal densities. Extensive evaluations reveal two critical failure modes in current paradigms: end-to-end MLLMs suffer from a Working Memory Bottleneck due to context saturation, while representative agentic baselines experience Global Localization Collapse when navigating sparse, month-long timelines. To address this, we propose the Recursive Multimodal Agent (ReMA), which employs dynamic memory management to iteratively update a recursive belief state, significantly outperforming existing methods. Finally, we establish dataset splits designed to isolate temporal and domain biases, providing a rigorous foundation for future research in supervised learning and out-of-distribution generalization.
memory.why_now: Long-horizon multimodal agents are a live topic, and this paper gives a concrete dataset plus failure taxonomy for what breaks when models must understand everyday life over days to months. The working-memory/context-saturation angle is timely because many current multimodal systems still rely on brute-force context stuffing.
memory.why_not_higher: It is still mainly a vision dataset-and-baseline paper, not a result that directly changes how mainstream AI systems are trained or served. The memory story is about agentic context management, not a concrete cache, offload, bandwidth, or storage mechanism.
downgrade_reasons: Memory connection is adjacent, not storage-systems core
Primary contribution is dataset plus benchmark baseline
Transfer beyond long-form video understanding is not yet established
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger cross-domain result showing the recursive memory scheme improving broader multimodal agents or reducing long-context inference cost across model families would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do multimodal models lose the plot over a month of daily life, and can recursive memory keep long-video agents from collapsing under their own context window?

memory.title: DARK: Diagonal-Anchored Repulsive Knowledge Distillation for Vision-Language Models under Extreme Compression
memory.abstract: Compressing vision-language models for on-device deployment is increasingly important in clinical settings, but knowledge distillation (KD) degrades sharply when the teacher-student capacity gap spans an order of magnitude or more. We argue that, under such gaps, strict imitation of the teacher is a poor objective: much of the teacher's pairwise similarity structure reflects its own architectural biases rather than information a compact student can efficiently represent. We propose \textbf{Diagonal-Anchored Repulsive Knowledge Distillation (DARK)}, a contrastive KD framework that decomposes the distillation loss into a diagonal term (matched image-text pairs) and an off-diagonal term (non-target similarities). The diagonal term anchors matched-pair alignment throughout training; the off-diagonal term is annealed from positive to negative weighting, transitioning the student from imitating to \emph{repelling} the teacher's non-target similarity structure. We instantiate DARK by distilling FetalCLIP, a 427M-parameter fetal ultrasound vision-language model, into \textbf{MobileFetalCLIP}, a 75M-parameter student model with a $26\times$ smaller visual encoder, running in 1.6\,ms on an iPhone~16~Pro. The student matches or exceeds its teacher on three zero-shot benchmarks, including HC18 biometry validity (88.6\% vs.\ 83.5\%) and brain sub-plane F1 (0.784 vs.\ 0.702). Embedding-geometry and logit analyses show that DARK induces \emph{structured decorrelation}: the student preserves teacher-aligned per-image confidence while diverging from inherited inter-class confusion, suggesting that controlled repulsion can be more efficient than imitation under extreme compression.
memory.why_now: On-device multimodal deployment is active right now, and this paper offers a crisp claim: under extreme compression, a student may do better by preserving matched pairs while actively unlearning the teacher's off-target confusions. The iPhone 16 Pro runtime result gives the idea a concrete edge-deployment story rather than a purely abstract KD tweak.
memory.why_not_higher: The evidence is still concentrated in one medical VLM stack, so it is not clear the method transfers to mainstream CLIP-, SigLIP-, or LLaVA-style compression. The memory angle is indirect because this is compression for deployment, not a direct cache, bandwidth, paging, or offload paper.
downgrade_reasons: Validation is confined to a fetal-ultrasound vision-language model
Memory/storage relevance is adjacent rather than core
Generality across public multimodal benchmarks and architectures is unproven
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A replication on widely used public VLMs with explicit RAM, latency, and energy tradeoffs across multiple edge devices would raise it substantially.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if tiny multimodal models get better not by copying their teachers more faithfully, but by learning which teacher confusions to actively reject?

memory.title: STRUCTUREDAGENT: Planning with AND/OR Trees for Long-Horizon Web Tasks
memory.abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems for sequential decision-making. Such agents must perceive their environment, reason across multiple time steps, and take actions that optimize long-term objectives. However, existing web agents struggle on complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that lead to premature termination. To address these challenges, we propose STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner that uses dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks. The framework also produces interpretable hierarchical plans, enabling easier debugging and facilitating human intervention when needed. Our results on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks show that STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.
memory.why_now: Web agents are still failing on long-horizon tasks, and this paper targets a live pain point: planning over many steps without collapsing into greedy behavior. The AND/OR-tree framing plus explicit candidate-tracking memory is timely because agent reliability and debuggability are active concerns.
memory.why_not_higher: The memory piece is agent-state bookkeeping, not a direct contribution to KV cache, bandwidth, offload, paging, or other first-class AI memory systems problems. It also reads more like a stronger task-specific agent framework than a result that broadly changes how modern AI systems are built or served.
downgrade_reasons: memory link is adjacent rather than core
web-agent benchmark focus is relatively narrow
limited evidence of transfer beyond browser task environments
likely more useful as an agent-method update than a top-tier episode anchor
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Show robust gains across broader agent settings and make the structured memory mechanism clearly transferable beyond web benchmarks into general LLM agent architectures.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do web agents fall apart on long tasks, and can tree-based planning plus explicit structured memory finally keep them on track?

monitor.title: Real-Time AI Service Economy: A Framework for Agentic Computing Across the Continuum
monitor.abstract: Real-time AI services increasingly operate across the device-edge-cloud continuum, where autonomous AI agents generate latency-sensitive workloads, orchestrate multi-stage processing pipelines, and compete for shared resources under policy and governance constraints. This article shows that the structure of service-dependency graphs, modelled as DAGs whose nodes represent compute stages and whose edges encode execution ordering, is a primary determinant of whether decentralised, price-based resource allocation can work reliably at scale. When dependency graphs are hierarchical (tree or series-parallel), prices converge to stable equilibria, optimal allocations can be computed efficiently, and under appropriate mechanism design (with quasilinear utilities and discrete slice items), agents have no incentive to misreport their valuations within each decision epoch. When dependencies are more complex, with cross-cutting ties between pipeline stages, prices oscillate, allocation quality degrades, and the system becomes difficult to manage. To bridge this gap, we propose a hybrid management architecture in which cross-domain integrators encapsulate complex sub-graphs into resource slices that present a simpler, well-structured interface to the rest of the market. A systematic ablation study across six experiments (1,620 runs, 10 seeds each) confirms that (i) dependency-graph topology is a first-order determinant of price stability and scalability,(ii) the hybrid architecture reduces price volatility by up to 70-75% without sacrificing throughput, (iii) governance constraints create quantifiable efficiency-compliance trade-offs that depend jointly on topology and load, and (iv) under truthful bidding the decentralised market matches a centralised value-optimal baseline, confirming that decentralised coordination can replicate centralised allocation quality.
monitor.why_now: Agentic workflows are pushing AI serving across device, edge, and cloud, so coordination and resource allocation are becoming real infrastructure problems. The timely idea here is that service-graph topology, not just pricing policy, determines whether decentralized AI markets stay stable.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is indirect, and the abstract does not show concrete LLM-serving, cache, or hardware results. This reads more like a simulation-heavy orchestration and mechanism-design framework than a deployment-proven AI systems paper.
downgrade_reasons: Weak direct memory/storage mechanism
No clear real-model or real-hardware evaluation in the abstract
Broad AI relevance is moderate rather than urgent
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A real deployment on LLM or multimodal serving stacks with measured latency, throughput, cost, and memory-pressure tradeoffs would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the make-or-break factor for agentic AI infrastructure is not the model, but whether the service graph is simple enough for decentralized resource markets to converge?

monitor.title: DiSCTT: Consensus-Guided Self-Curriculum for Efficient Test-Time Adaptation in Reasoning
monitor.abstract: Test-time adaptation offers a promising avenue for improving reasoning performance in large language models without additional supervision, but existing approaches often apply a uniform optimization objective across all inputs, leading to inefficient or unstable adaptation on heterogeneous reasoning problems. We propose DiSCTT, a difficulty-aware, consensus-guided self-curriculum framework that dynamically allocates test-time optimization strategies based on instance-level epistemic uncertainty estimated from agreement among sampled reasoning trajectories. Inputs with high consensus are consolidated via supervised fine-tuning using majority-agreed solutions as pseudo-labels, while low-consensus inputs are optimized via reinforcement learning with a consensus-regularized objective that encourages diversity under relevance constraints. Across a broad suite of mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, DiSCTT consistently outperforms strong test-time adaptation baselines, achieving higher accuracy with reduced variance and substantially lower computation and wall-clock training times. These results demonstrate that explicitly accounting for instance difficulty and uncertainty enables more stable, efficient, and effective test-time adaptation for reasoning models.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning-time compute is a live topic, and this paper targets a real pain point: spending the same adaptation budget on every prompt is wasteful and unstable. A difficulty-aware test-time curriculum is relevant now because people are actively looking for cheaper, more reliable ways to improve reasoning without new labeled data.
monitor.why_not_higher: The abstract still reads mostly like a benchmark-method result rather than a clear shift in how frontier systems will be built or served. It also has no real memory/storage mechanism, so it only fits one of the podcast's two lenses.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Crowded test-time reasoning optimization area
Production-scale serving relevance is not yet clear
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show consistent gains on widely used frontier reasoning models under explicit latency and cost budgets, with a deployment recipe that clearly beats simpler test-time compute policies.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if a reasoning model could decide, problem by problem, when to trust consensus and when to spend extra test-time learning before it answers?

monitor.title: Towards Provably Unbiased LLM Judges via Bias-Bounded Evaluation
monitor.abstract: As AI models progress beyond simple chatbots into more complex workflows, we draw ever closer to the event horizon beyond which AI systems will be utilized in autonomous, self-maintaining feedback loops. Any autonomous AI system will depend on automated, verifiable rewards and feedback; in settings where ground truth is sparse or non-deterministic, one practical source of such rewards is an LLM-as-a-Judge. Although LLM judges continue to improve, the literature has yet to introduce systems capable of enforcing standards with strong guarantees, particularly when bias vectors are unknown or adversarially discovered. To remedy this issue, we propose average bias-boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework which formally guarantees reductions of harm/impact as a result of any measurable bias in an LLM judge. Evaluating on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges, we achieve (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings, with most judge-bias combinations exceeding 80%. The code to reproduce our findings is available at https://github.com/penfever/bias-bounded-evaluation.
monitor.why_now: LLM-as-a-judge is becoming core infrastructure for evals, agents, and automated feedback loops, so a paper claiming bias-bounded guarantees lands at the right moment. The trustworthiness of AI evaluators is moving from an alignment side topic into an operational concern.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is absent, and the empirical validation is still centered on judge benchmarks and constructed bias settings rather than broad production eval workloads. It is more about making evaluation safer than changing how models are trained or served.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Evaluation is limited to LLM-judge benchmark settings
Practical deployment impact is not yet proven across real eval pipelines
