monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show the method holding up across diverse real-world evaluation pipelines and materially changing how frontier labs or model platforms run automated judging.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can we make AI judges reliable enough to grade other AIs without quietly importing hidden bias into the loop?

monitor.title: Improved Scaling Laws via Weak-to-Strong Generalization in Random Feature Ridge Regression
monitor.abstract: It is increasingly common in machine learning to use learned models to label data and then employ such data to train more capable models. The phenomenon of weak-to-strong generalization exemplifies the advantage of this two-stage procedure: a strong student is trained on imperfect labels obtained from a weak teacher, and yet the strong student outperforms the weak teacher. In this paper, we show that the potential improvement is substantial, in the sense that it affects the scaling law followed by the test error. Specifically, we consider students and teachers trained via random feature ridge regression (RFRR). Our main technical contribution is to derive a deterministic equivalent for the excess test error of the student trained on labels obtained via the teacher. Via this deterministic equivalent, we then identify regimes in which the scaling law of the student improves upon that of the teacher, unveiling that the improvement can be achieved both in bias-dominated and variance-dominated settings. Strikingly, the student may attain the minimax optimal rate regardless of the scaling law of the teacher -- in fact, when the test error of the teacher does not even decay with the sample size.
monitor.why_now: Model-labeled data, self-training, and weak-to-strong transfer are active topics as AI labs lean harder on synthetic supervision. A paper arguing that this can improve the scaling law itself, not just the constant factor, is timely.
monitor.why_not_higher: The core result is proved in a random feature ridge regression setting, so the jump to frontier LLM training remains indirect. There is no direct memory/systems contribution and no validation on realistic modern workloads.
downgrade_reasons: Stylized theory setting rather than modern LLM experiments
No direct memory/storage relevance
Unclear near-term impact on practitioner decisions
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Empirical evidence that the predicted scaling-law gains appear in realistic teacher-student training pipelines for modern foundation models would raise it materially.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a student trained on flawed labels from a weaker model actually beat its teacher so decisively that it changes the learning curve itself?

monitor.title: Stable-LoRA: Stabilizing Feature Learning of Low-Rank Adaptation
monitor.abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is a widely adopted parameter-efficient method for fine-tuning Large Langauge Models. It updates the weight matrix as $W=W_0+sBA$, where $W_0$ is the original frozen weight, $s$ is a scaling factor and $A$,$B$ are trainable low-rank matrices. Despite its robust empirical effectiveness, the theoretical foundations of LoRA remain insufficiently understood, particularly with respect to feature learning stability. In this paper, we first establish that, LoRA can, in principle, naturally achieve and sustain stable feature learning (i.e., be self-stabilized) under appropriate hyper-parameters and initializations of $A$ and $B$. However, we also uncover a fundamental limitation that the necessary non-zero initialization of $A$ compromises self-stability, leading to suboptimal performances. To address this challenge, we propose Stable-LoRA, a weight-shrinkage optimization strategy that dynamically enhances stability of LoRA feature learning. By progressively shrinking $A$ during the earliest training steps, Stable-LoRA is both theoretically and empirically validated to effectively eliminate instability of LoRA feature learning while preserving the benefits of the non-zero start. Experiments show that Stable-LoRA consistently outperforms other baselines across diverse models and tasks, with no additional memory usage and only negligible computation overheads. The code is available at https://github.com/Yize-Wu/Stable-LoRA.
monitor.why_now: LoRA remains a default fine-tuning path for open models, so a simple stabilization trick that improves training without extra memory could matter quickly if the gains hold up. The paper also offers a cleaner explanation of when LoRA is stable versus fragile.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage angle is not central, and the abstract does not yet show a systems-level shift beyond better LoRA training behavior. It reads more like a useful refinement to a common recipe than a clear step-change result.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is weak
Impact appears limited to LoRA fine-tuning workflows
Effect size and workload realism are unclear from the abstract
Crowded PEFT and optimizer space
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Clear wins on mainstream instruction-tuning or preference-tuning workloads at meaningful model scales, plus fast independent replication, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: LoRA made fine-tuning cheap, but why can it still learn unstable features, and can a tiny early-training fix make it reliably behave?

monitor.title: Emergent Introspection in AI is Content-Agnostic
monitor.abstract: Introspection is a foundational cognitive ability, but its mechanism is not well understood. Recent work has shown that AI models can introspect. We study the mechanism of this introspection. We first extensively replicate Lindsey (2025)'s thought injection detection paradigm in large open-source models. We show that introspection in these models is content-agnostic: models can detect that an anomaly occurred even when they cannot reliably identify its content. The models confabulate injected concepts that are high-frequency and concrete (e.g., "apple"). They also require fewer tokens to detect an injection than to guess the correct concept (with wrong guesses coming earlier). We argue that a content-agnostic introspective mechanism is consistent with leading theories in philosophy and psychology.
monitor.why_now: As labs push models to self-monitor and explain their own reasoning, this paper tests whether apparent introspection is real understanding or just anomaly detection. That matters for how seriously people should take model self-reports in evals and safety discussions.
monitor.why_not_higher: The result is conceptually interesting but operationally narrow, with no direct path to changes in training, serving, or memory systems. The evidence comes from a synthetic thought-injection paradigm rather than realistic agent or production workloads.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage angle
Limited impact on how systems are built or served
Synthetic evaluation setup limits transfer
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the same effect materially changes how frontier models should be evaluated in real agent, tool-use, or safety-monitoring settings.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if language models can tell that a foreign thought slipped in before they can say what it was, and their confident answers are just confabulations around a detected glitch?

monitor.title: Revisiting the (Sub)Optimality of Best-of-N for Inference-Time Alignment
monitor.abstract: Best-of-N (BoN) sampling is a widely used inference-time alignment method for language models, whereby N candidate responses are sampled from a reference model and the one with the highest predicted reward according to a learned reward model is selected. Despite its widespread practical use, recent theoretical work has suggested that it is statistically suboptimal and vulnerable to reward hacking, the process by which models exploit weaknesses in the learned reward model to achieve high estimated reward without genuinely improving performance. We revisit this question under assumptions that more closely reflect practice than that of prior work. In particular, in contradistinction to earlier analyses that focused on expected true reward, which may not be meaningful in many practical settings, we investigate how inference-time alignment affects the win-rate, a pairwise comparison-based metric more closely aligned with how reward models are trained and evaluated in practice. We demonstrate that, under minimal conditions on the quality of the reference model and learned reward model, properly tuned BoN is both computationally and statistically optimal in achieving high win-rate, partially explaining its widespread practical success. Because BoN remains susceptible to reward-hacking in this setting, we propose a simple and practical variant that provably eliminates reward-hacking while maintaining optimal statistical performance. Finally, we show that prior approaches are provably suboptimal when considering win-rate, highlighting the importance of choosing appropriate objectives when analyzing inference-time alignment methods.
monitor.why_now: Best-of-N remains a standard inference-time alignment baseline, and this paper pushes back on the claim that it is inherently suboptimal by arguing that win-rate is the right practical objective. That makes it timely for teams tuning LLM sampling, reranking, and reward-model-based evaluation.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is still mainly a theory-and-evaluation paper rather than a clear systems or product shift, and the abstract does not show broad validation on frontier production workloads. It also has no meaningful memory/storage angle.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily theory/evaluation rather than deployment systems
Unclear empirical breadth on frontier models
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Large-scale empirical evidence that the reward-hacking-free variant improves real user-facing win-rate at practical inference budgets would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why does Best-of-N keep working in practice when theory says it should be suboptimal, and can we keep the gains without reward hacking?

monitor.title: Autonomous Algorithm Discovery for Ptychography via Evolutionary LLM Reasoning
monitor.abstract: Ptychography is a computational imaging technique widely used for high-resolution materials characterization, but high-quality reconstructions often require the use of regularization functions that largely remain manually designed. We introduce Ptychi-Evolve, an autonomous framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to discover and evolve novel regularization algorithms. The framework combines LLM-driven code generation with evolutionary mechanisms, including semantically-guided crossover and mutation. Experiments on three challenging datasets (X-ray integrated circuits, low-dose electron microscopy of apoferritin, and multislice imaging with crosstalk artifacts) demonstrate that discovered regularizers outperform conventional reconstructions, achieving up to +0.26 SSIM and +8.3~dB PSNR improvements. Besides, Ptychi-Evolve records algorithm lineage and evolution metadata, enabling interpretable and reproducible analysis of discovered regularizers.
monitor.why_now: Autonomous algorithm discovery is a live AI-for-science theme, and this paper shows an LLM-plus-evolution loop producing measurable gains rather than just generating ideas. The code-level search and lineage tracking make it more concrete than many agentic-discovery claims.
monitor.why_not_higher: The actual contribution is still a niche ptychography reconstruction result, not a change to how mainstream AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated. The memory/storage connection is absent, and broad transfer beyond this imaging setting is not yet established.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow domain-specific reconstruction task
No direct impact on modern AI system practice
Memory/storage relevance is absent
General transfer is not convincingly demonstrated
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate the same framework discovering reusable algorithms across multiple domains or core AI systems tasks, not just ptychographic reconstruction.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an LLM-guided evolutionary loop actually invent better scientific algorithms than humans, or is this just a strong result in one very specialized imaging pipeline?

monitor.title: Designing Explainable Conversational Agentic Systems for Guaraní Speakers
monitor.abstract: Although artificial intelligence (AI) and Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) systems are often presented as universal solutions, their design remains predominantly text-first, underserving primarily oral languages and indigenous communities. This position paper uses Guaraní, an official and widely spoken language of Paraguay, as a case study to argue that language support in AI remains insufficient unless it aligns with lived oral practices. We propose an alternative to the standard "text-to-speech" pipeline, proposing instead an oral-first multi-agent architecture. By decoupling Guaraní natural language understanding from dedicated agents for conversation state and community-led governance, we demonstrate a technical framework that respects indigenous data sovereignty and diglossia. Our work moves beyond mere recognition to focus on turn-taking, repair, and shared context as the primary locus of interaction. We conclude that for AI to be truly culturally grounded, it must shift from adapting oral languages to text-centric systems to treating spoken conversation as a first-class design requirement, ensuring digital ecosystems empower rather than overlook diverse linguistic practices.
monitor.why_now: As voice agents and conversational AI become more prominent, this paper argues that text-first assumptions still break badly for oral-language communities. That framing is timely and could broaden how people think about what 'language support' in AI really means.
monitor.why_not_higher: This reads as a position paper more than a validated systems result, with little evidence on realistic deployments, model behavior, or measurable gains. It is also centered on a specific sociolinguistic context, so the near-term impact on mainstream AI building is limited.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited empirical support
Narrow case-study framing for this cycle
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up with deployed oral-first agents, concrete evaluations against text-first baselines, and evidence that the design generalizes beyond Guarani would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if voice AI fails oral-language communities not because speech models are weak, but because the whole stack still treats text as the real interface?

monitor.title: The Rise of AI in Weather and Climate Information and its Impact on Global Inequality
monitor.abstract: The rapid adoption of AI in Earth system science promises unprecedented speed and fidelity in the generation of climate information. However, this technological prowess rests on a fragile and unequal foundation: the current trajectory of AI development risks further automating and amplifying the North-South divide in the global climate information system. We outline the global asymmetry in High-Performance Computing and data infrastructure, demonstrating that the development of foundation models is almost exclusively concentrated in the Global North. Using three different domains, we show how this infrastructure inequality continues through models' inputs, processes and outputs. As an example, in weather and climate modelling, the reliance on historically biased data leads to systematic performance gaps that disproportionately affect the most vulnerable regions. In climate impact modelling, data sparsity and unrepresentative validation risk driving misleading interventions and maladaptation. Finally, in large language models, dependence on dominant textualised forms of climate knowledge risks reinforcing existing biases. We conclude that addressing these disparities demands revisiting the three phases, i.e. models Input, Process and Output. This involves (i) a perspective shift from model-centric to data-centric development, (ii) the establishment of a Climate Digital Public Infrastructure and human-centric evaluation metrics, and (iii) a move from producer-consumer dynamics toward knowledge co-production. This integration of diverse knowledge systems would truly democratise compute sovereignty and ensure that the AI revolution fosters genuine systemic resilience rather than exacerbating inequity.
monitor.why_now: AI weather and climate models are moving from research into real decision pipelines, so the paper's argument that compute, data, and evaluation are reproducing North-South inequality is timely. It also speaks to the current push toward foundation models for science and who gets to build and benefit from them.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is mostly a framing and synthesis paper rather than a concrete systems or model paper with strong new empirical validation. It also has no direct memory/storage mechanism, so it does not advance the podcast's second lens.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage contribution
Mostly perspective/synthesis rather than new technical results
Limited evidence on realistic AI system interventions
Climate-domain focus narrows transfer to mainstream AI builders
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up with hard cross-region benchmarks and concrete data or infrastructure interventions that measurably reduce performance inequality would raise priority.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if AI makes climate forecasting more powerful overall, but quietly entrenches a global compute and data order that leaves the most vulnerable regions worse served?

monitor.title: WebChain: A Large-Scale Human-Annotated Dataset of Real-World Web Interaction Traces
monitor.abstract: We introduce WebChain, the largest open-source dataset of human-annotated trajectories on real-world websites, designed to accelerate reproducible research in web agents. It contains 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring a core Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data to provide rich, multi-modal supervision. The data is collected via a scalable pipeline that ensures coverage of complex, high-value tasks often missed by synthetic methods. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on our proposed WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks. Our work provides the data and insights necessary to build and rigorously evaluate the next generation of scalable web agents.
monitor.why_now: Web agents are moving from demo territory into real product interest, but open training and evaluation data for messy real websites is still thin. A large human-annotated dataset and a training recipe that improves GUI benchmarks land at the right moment for that discussion.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is still mainly a web-agent dataset and benchmark paper, not a clear shift in how the broader field builds or serves modern AI systems. The episode value depends heavily on framing it as part of the web-agent data bottleneck rather than as a standalone leaderboard story.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage contribution
Dataset-and-benchmark heavy
Transferability beyond web and GUI agents is still uncertain
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Clear evidence that WebChain-trained agents materially improve real production agent workflows outside benchmark suites would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: If web agents keep breaking on real websites because their training data is too synthetic, WebChain argues the missing ingredient may be large-scale human traces of how people actually use the web.

monitor.title: DepthCharge: A Domain-Agnostic Framework for Measuring Depth-Dependent Knowledge in Large Language Models
monitor.abstract: Large Language Models appear competent when answering general questions but often fail when pushed into domain-specific details. No existing methodology provides an out-of-the-box solution for measuring how deeply LLMs can sustain accurate responses under adaptive follow-up questioning across arbitrary domains.   We present DepthCharge, a domain-agnostic framework that measures knowledge depth through three innovations: adaptive probing that generates follow-up questions based on concepts the model actually mentions, on-demand fact verification from authoritative sources, and survival statistics with constant sample sizes at every depth level. The framework can be deployed on any knowledge domain with publicly verifiable facts, without requiring pre-constructed test sets or domain-specific expertise. DepthCharge results are relative to the evaluator model used for answer checking, making the framework a tool for comparative evaluation rather than absolute accuracy certification.   Empirical validation across four diverse domains (Medicine, Constitutional Law, Ancient Rome, and Quantum Computing) with five frontier models demonstrates that DepthCharge reveals depth-dependent performance variation hidden by standard benchmarks. Expected Valid Depth (EVD) ranges from 3.45 to 7.55 across model-domain combinations, and model rankings vary substantially by domain, with no single model dominating all areas. Cost-performance analysis further reveals that expensive models do not always achieve deeper knowledge, suggesting that domain-specific evaluation is more informative than aggregate benchmarks for model selection in professional applications.
monitor.why_now: LLM buyers keep learning that average benchmark scores hide domain-specific failure modes, and this paper offers a reusable way to probe where answers start to break under follow-up pressure. That is timely for professional deployments where shallow competence is not enough.
monitor.why_not_higher: The contribution is an evaluation framework, not a new capability or systems method, so the episode risks becoming benchmark commentary. Its results are also explicitly relative to the evaluator model, which limits how definitive the rankings feel.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage contribution
Benchmark-centric rather than a build/train/serve change
Evaluator-relative verification limits absolute confidence
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger real-world case showing that DepthCharge-driven model selection reduced failures in a deployed professional workflow would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: How many follow-up questions can an LLM survive before its expertise falls apart, and why do standard benchmarks miss that cliff?

monitor.title: X-RAY: Mapping LLM Reasoning Capability via Formalized and Calibrated Probes
monitor.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve promising performance, yet their ability to reason remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations largely emphasize task-level accuracy, often conflating pattern matching with reasoning capability. We present X-RAY, an explainable reasoning analysis system that maps the LLM reasoning capability using calibrated, formally verified probes. We model reasoning capability as a function of extractable \textit{structure}, operationalized through formal properties such as constraint interaction, reasoning depth, and solution-space geometry. X-Ray generates probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations, enabling precise isolation of incremental structural information through formal calibration and verification. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on problems ranging from junior-level to advanced in mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our analysis reveals a systematic asymmetry in LLM reasoning: models are relatively robust to constraint refinement, where additional conditions shrink an existing solution space, but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring, where modifications alter the underlying structural form of the solution manifold. Moreover, calibrated formal probes differentiate models that appear indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal failure modes that are structurally interpretable rather than opaque. Beyond evaluation, our framework is contamination-free and supports the training and testing of reasoning models.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning remains a central claim for frontier LLMs, and this paper offers a cleaner way to separate real structural reasoning from benchmark pattern matching. Its calibrated, formally verified probes also speak to current concern about contaminated or overly gameable reasoning evals.
monitor.why_not_higher: The contribution is mostly evaluative rather than a change to how models are built, trained, or served in production. The memory/storage lens is absent, and the practical payoff depends on whether the framework gets adopted beyond benchmark analysis.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Primarily an evaluation framework, not a systems or training advance
Operational impact on day-to-day model building is still indirect
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show that X-RAY predicts real deployment-relevant reasoning failures or becomes a standard evaluation target for frontier reasoning models.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if today's reasoning benchmarks are grading LLMs on pattern recall, while X-RAY exposes the exact structural changes that make their reasoning break?

monitor.title: On the Generalization Capacities of MLLMs for Spatial Intelligence
monitor.abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) that directly process RGB inputs for tasks like 3D localization and navigation have shown remarkable potential. However, we argue that these RGB-only approaches are fundamentally flawed in their ability to generalize across cameras. By ignoring camera parameters, they entangle an object's physical properties with the camera's perspective, creating an irresolvable ambiguity. We show this leads MLLMs to overfit to the training camera distribution, rather than learning true and generalizable 3D geometric principles. To address this, we propose Camera-Aware MLLM framework for spatial MLLMs. It learns generalizable spatial reasoning by: (i) injecting camera intrinsics via a dense embedding that conditions each visual token; (ii) introducing a camera-aware data augmentation strategy that synthetically varies camera parameters, forcing the model to disentangle camera properties from scene content; and (iii) distilling geometric priors from a 3D vision foundation model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that camera-aware MLLMs substantially outperform their naive counterparts, particularly in cross-camera generalization tests on spatially-grounded tasks, indicating that camera-awareness is not only beneficial but also a prerequisite for robust and generalizable spatial intelligence in MLLMs.
monitor.why_now: Spatial MLLMs and embodied VLMs are being pushed into navigation and 3D tasks right now, and this paper identifies a concrete failure mode in RGB-only training. The camera-aware framing is a useful warning for anyone making strong spatial-reasoning claims about multimodal models.
monitor.why_not_higher: The result is still centered on a specific spatial-vision setup rather than a change most AI builders need to adopt immediately. It has essentially no memory/storage relevance, and the jump from benchmark gains to production robotics or agent stacks is not yet established.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection absent
Primarily a spatial-MLLM training and evaluation paper
Unclear transfer beyond embodied or 3D vision workloads
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same camera-aware recipe consistently improving major VLMs on real robot, navigation, or cross-device spatial workloads.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do multimodal models seem to understand 3D until you swap the camera, and what does that reveal about how shallow their spatial intelligence still is?

monitor.title: Med-V1: Small Language Models for Zero-shot and Scalable Biomedical Evidence Attribution
monitor.abstract: Assessing whether an article supports an assertion is essential for hallucination detection and claim verification. While large language models (LLMs) have the potential to automate this task, achieving strong performance requires frontier models such as GPT-5 that are prohibitively expensive to deploy at scale. To efficiently perform biomedical evidence attribution, we present Med-V1, a family of small language models with only three billion parameters. Trained on high-quality synthetic data newly developed in this study, Med-V1 substantially outperforms (+27.0% to +71.3%) its base models on five biomedical benchmarks unified into a verification format. Despite its smaller size, Med-V1 performs comparably to frontier LLMs such as GPT-5, along with high-quality explanations for its predictions. We use Med-V1 to conduct a first-of-its-kind use case study that quantifies hallucinations in LLM-generated answers under different citation instructions. Results show that the format instruction strongly affects citation validity and hallucination, with GPT-5 generating more claims but exhibiting hallucination rates similar to GPT-4o. Additionally, we present a second use case showing that Med-V1 can automatically identify high-stakes evidence misattributions in clinical practice guidelines, revealing potentially negative public health impacts that are otherwise challenging to identify at scale. Overall, Med-V1 provides an efficient and accurate lightweight alternative to frontier LLMs for practical and real-world applications in biomedical evidence attribution and verification tasks. Med-V1 is available at https://github.com/ncbi-nlp/Med-V1.
monitor.why_now: Hallucination auditing and citation validity are immediate problems for LLM deployment, and this paper argues a 3B domain model can do high-stakes biomedical verification at frontier-model quality for much lower cost. That is timely for teams trying to make medical LLM use safer and cheaper in practice.
monitor.why_not_higher: The contribution is still centered on a narrow biomedical evidence-attribution task rather than a general change in how mainstream AI systems are built, served, or evaluated. The transfer story outside biomed and outside this verification setup is plausible but not yet established.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage contribution
Narrow biomedical task focus
Limited evidence of transfer beyond evidence attribution
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A strong cross-domain result showing the same small-model verifier recipe works outside biomed or inside real production evaluation pipelines would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the cheapest way to catch frontier-model hallucinations in medicine is a specialized 3B model built to verify whether the cited evidence actually supports the claim?

monitor.title: Censored LLMs as a Natural Testbed for Secret Knowledge Elicitation
monitor.abstract: Large language models sometimes produce false or misleading responses. Two approaches to this problem are honesty elicitation -- modifying prompts or weights so that the model answers truthfully -- and lie detection -- classifying whether a given response is false. Prior work evaluates such methods on models specifically trained to lie or conceal information, but these artificial constructions may not resemble naturally-occurring dishonesty. We instead study open-weights LLMs from Chinese developers, which are trained to censor politically sensitive topics: Qwen3 models frequently produce falsehoods about subjects like Falun Gong or the Tiananmen protests while occasionally answering correctly, indicating they possess knowledge they are trained to suppress. Using this as a testbed, we evaluate a suite of elicitation and lie detection techniques. For honesty elicitation, sampling without a chat template, few-shot prompting, and fine-tuning on generic honesty data most reliably increase truthful responses. For lie detection, prompting the censored model to classify its own responses performs near an uncensored-model upper bound, and linear probes trained on unrelated data offer a cheaper alternative. The strongest honesty elicitation techniques also transfer to frontier open-weights models including DeepSeek R1. Notably, no technique fully eliminates false responses. We release all prompts, code, and transcripts.
monitor.why_now: As open-weight frontier models spread, it matters whether they contain knowledge they are trained to suppress and whether simple prompting or fine-tuning can recover it. Using Qwen-style censorship as a natural dishonesty testbed is timely because it is closer to real deployed behavior than toy models trained to lie.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is still mostly an evaluation and elicitation study rather than a clear new recipe that reliably fixes honesty failures in production systems. The strongest evidence is concentrated in politically censored topics, so generality to other kinds of hidden or deceptive model knowledge remains only partly shown.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Primarily a benchmark and elicitation paper rather than a new system
Generality beyond politically censored domains is limited
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Broader cross-model results on non-political hidden-knowledge settings, plus a method that consistently cuts false answers without major tradeoffs, would move this into cover-now territory.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if a language model already knows the truth but its training has taught it to lie on cue, and can we make it tell on itself?

monitor.title: Thinking with Spatial Code for Physical-World Video Reasoning
monitor.abstract: We introduce Thinking with Spatial Code, a framework that transforms RGB video into explicit, temporally coherent 3D representations for physical-world visual question answering. We highlight the empirical finding that our proposed spatial encoder can parse videos into structured spatial code with explicit 3D oriented bounding boxes and semantic labels, enabling large language models (LLMs) to reason directly over explicit spatial variables. Specifically, we propose the spatial encoder that encodes image and geometric features by unifying 6D object parsing and tracking backbones with geometric prediction, and we further finetuning LLMs with reinforcement learning using a spatial rubric reward that encourages perspective-aware, geometrically grounded inference. As a result, our model outperforms proprietary vision-language models on VSI-Bench, setting a new state-of-the-art. Code is available at https://github.com/Beckschen/spatialcode.
monitor.why_now: It taps into a live question for multimodal AI: whether explicit structured world models can help LLMs reason about video better than end-to-end vision-language stacks. The combination of 3D scene parsing, temporal coherence, and language reasoning makes it timely for people watching physical-world and embodied AI.
monitor.why_not_higher: The paper still looks anchored to a specific physical-world VQA benchmark rather than showing a clear shift in how mainstream AI systems are built or served. Its memory/storage relevance is effectively absent, and the transfer beyond this task framing is not yet proven.
downgrade_reasons: memory/storage connection is absent
benchmark-centric evidence with unclear real-world deployment path
transferability beyond physical-world video QA is not yet established
may be a narrow task paper despite broad reasoning language
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that spatial code improves multiple open-ended video or embodied reasoning settings, not just a single benchmark, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if video reasoning gets better when we stop asking models to infer physics from pixels alone and instead hand them an explicit 3D world to think with?

monitor.title: Towards Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation Based on Knowledge Graph: A Comparative Analysis
monitor.abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) was introduced to enhance the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) beyond their encoded prior knowledge. This is achieved by providing LLMs with an external source of knowledge, which helps reduce factual hallucinations and enables access to new information not available during pretraining. However, inconsistent retrieved information can negatively affect LLM responses. The Retrieval-Augmented Generation Benchmark (RGB) was introduced to evaluate the robustness of RAG systems under such conditions. In this work, we use the RGB corpus to evaluate LLMs in four scenarios: noise robustness, information integration, negative rejection, and counterfactual robustness. We perform a comparative analysis between the RGB RAG baseline and GraphRAG, a knowledge graph based retrieval system. We test three GraphRAG customizations to improve robustness. Results show improvements over the RGB baseline and provide insights for designing more reliable RAG systems for real world scenarios.
monitor.why_now: RAG reliability is still a live deployment problem, and this paper breaks that problem into concrete failure modes like noisy retrieval, negative rejection, and counterfactual contamination. The GraphRAG comparison is timely because many teams are still testing whether structured retrieval actually improves LLM reliability.
monitor.why_not_higher: This looks more like a useful comparative benchmark paper than a clear step-change in how people build LLM systems. The evidence also appears centered on RGB-style evaluation rather than broad real-world deployments across retrieval stacks and models.
downgrade_reasons: Weak memory/storage relevance
Benchmark-centric evidence
Incremental relative to existing RAG robustness work
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show consistent gains on multiple real-world corpora and mainstream LLM stacks, with concrete deployment guidance that beats standard vector RAG beyond a single benchmark setup.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: If RAG breaks when retrieval gets noisy or contradictory, does GraphRAG actually make LLM answers more trustworthy, or just look better on a benchmark?

monitor.title: Berta: an open-source, modular tool for AI-enabled clinical documentation
monitor.abstract: Commercial AI scribes cost \$99-600 per physician per month, operate as opaque systems, and do not return data to institutional infrastructure, limiting organizational control over data governance, quality improvement, and clinical workflows. We developed Berta, an open-source modular scribe platform for AI-enabled clinical documentation, and deployed a customized implementation within Alberta Health Services (AHS) integrated with their existing Snowflake AI Data Cloud infrastructure. The system combines automatic speech recognition with large language models while retaining all clinical data within the secure AHS environment. During eight months (November 2024 to July 2025), 198 emergency physicians used the system in 105 urban and rural facilities, generating 22148 clinical sessions and more than 2800 hours of audio. The use grew from 680 to 5530 monthly sessions. Operating costs averaged less than \$30 per physician per month, a 70-95% reduction compared to commercial alternatives. AHS has since approved expansion to 850 physicians. This is the first provincial-scale deployment of an AI scribe integrated with existing health system infrastructure. By releasing Berta as open source, we provide a reproducible, cost-effective alternative that health systems can adapt to their own secure environments, supporting data sovereignty and informed evaluation of AI documentation technology.
monitor.why_now: This is a real, at-scale deployment story for LLM-based clinical documentation, with concrete cost, governance, and adoption data instead of a toy pilot. The open-source angle and secure in-house integration make it relevant as institutions are deciding whether to buy opaque AI tools or build controlled internal workflows.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is still a narrow clinical application paper, not a general advance in model design, training, inference, or evaluation. The memory/storage lens is essentially absent, and most of the value is operational deployment rather than a reusable AI systems technique.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow clinical documentation use case
No direct memory or storage mechanism
Limited transfer beyond regulated enterprise workflows
More deployment case study than core AI method advance
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger cross-domain lesson set on secure enterprise LLM deployment, with reusable design patterns or comparative evaluations that clearly transfer beyond healthcare, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What happens when a public health system builds its own AI scribe instead of renting a black box from a vendor?

monitor.title: TML-Bench: Benchmark for Data Science Agents on Tabular ML Tasks
monitor.abstract: Autonomous coding agents can produce strong tabular baselines quickly on Kaggle-style tasks. Practical value depends on end-to-end correctness and reliability under time limits. This paper introduces TML-Bench, a tabular benchmark for data science agents on Kaggle-style tasks. This paper evaluates 10 OSS LLMs on four Kaggle competitions and three time budgets (240s, 600s, and 1200s). Each model is run five times per task and budget. A run is successful if it produces a valid submission and a private-holdout score on hidden labels that are not accessible to the agent. This paper reports median performance, success rates, and run-to-run variability. MiniMax-M2.1 model achieves the best aggregate performance score on all four competitions under the paper's primary aggregation. Average performance improves with larger time budgets. Scaling is noisy for some individual models at the current run count. Code and materials are available at https://github.com/MykolaPinchuk/TML-bench/tree/master.
monitor.why_now: Coding and data science agents are being pitched as practical automation tools right now, so a benchmark that tests end-to-end reliability under real time budgets is timely. The hidden-label evaluation and valid-submission criterion make this more useful than a pure code-generation leaderboard.
monitor.why_not_higher: The scope is still narrow: four Kaggle-style tabular competitions are not the same thing as general ML engineering or production agent reliability. It is also mostly a benchmark result rather than a paper that changes how modern AI systems are built or served.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited transfer beyond Kaggle-style tabular tasks
Small task set and noisy scaling at current run count
More evaluation snapshot than strategic shift
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A broader benchmark spanning more tasks, stronger model coverage, human or frontier-agent baselines, and clearer evidence that results transfer to real production ML workflows would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Do data science agents actually handle the messy end-to-end reality of Kaggle-style ML, or do they still fall apart once hidden test labels and hard time limits enter the picture?

deferred.title: Bias In, Bias Out? Finding Unbiased Subnetworks in Vanilla Models
deferred.abstract: The issue of algorithmic biases in deep learning has led to the development of various debiasing techniques, many of which perform complex training procedures or dataset manipulation. However, an intriguing question arises: is it possible to extract fair and bias-agnostic subnetworks from standard vanilla-trained models without relying on additional data, such as unbiased training set? In this work, we introduce Bias-Invariant Subnetwork Extraction (BISE), a learning strategy that identifies and isolates "bias-free" subnetworks that already exist within conventionally trained models, without retraining or finetuning the original parameters. Our approach demonstrates that such subnetworks can be extracted via pruning and can operate without modification, effectively relying less on biased features and maintaining robust performance. Our findings contribute towards efficient bias mitigation through structural adaptation of pre-trained neural networks via parameter removal, as opposed to costly strategies that are either data-centric or involve (re)training all model parameters. Extensive experiments on common benchmarks show the advantages of our approach in terms of the performance and computational efficiency of the resulting debiased model.
deferred.why_now: The paper asks a timely practical question: can teams make already-trained models fairer without retraining or collecting new data? That post-hoc debiasing angle is relevant for people auditing deployed models under real cost constraints.
deferred.why_not_higher: The evidence appears centered on standard vision fairness benchmarks rather than modern large-model or production settings, and the memory/storage link is effectively absent. It reads more like an interesting niche debiasing result than a paper that changes mainstream AI system design.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Benchmark-heavy validation in likely narrow vision settings
Unclear impact on current large-model training or serving practice
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same pruning-based debiasing working convincingly on contemporary language or multimodal models with strong fairness gains and clear deployment tradeoffs.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you literally prune bias out of a trained model after the fact, or do the gains disappear once you leave the benchmark?

deferred.title: The Geometric Inductive Bias of Grokking: Bypassing Phase Transitions via Architectural Topology
deferred.abstract: Mechanistic interpretability typically relies on post-hoc analysis of trained networks. We instead adopt an interventional approach: testing hypotheses a priori by modifying architectural topology to observe training dynamics. We study grokking - delayed generalization in Transformers trained on cyclic modular addition (Zp) - investigating if specific architectural degrees of freedom prolong the memorization phase.   We identify two independent structural factors in standard Transformers: unbounded representational magnitude and data-dependent attention routing. First, we introduce a fully bounded spherical topology enforcing L2 normalization throughout the residual stream and an unembedding matrix with a fixed temperature scale. This removes magnitude-based degrees of freedom, reducing grokking onset time by over 20x without weight decay. Second, a Uniform Attention Ablation overrides data-dependent query-key routing with a uniform distribution, reducing the attention layer to a Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW) aggregator. Despite removing adaptive routing, these models achieve 100% generalization across all seeds and bypass the grokking delay entirely.   To evaluate whether this acceleration is a task-specific geometric alignment rather than a generic optimization stabilizer, we use non-commutative S5 permutation composition as a negative control. Enforcing spherical constraints on S5 does not accelerate generalization. This suggests eliminating the memorization phase depends strongly on aligning architectural priors with the task's intrinsic symmetries. Together, these findings provide interventional evidence that architectural degrees of freedom substantially influence grokking, suggesting a predictive structural perspective on training dynamics.
deferred.why_now: It gives a clean interventional story for grokking: architectural geometry and attention topology can erase delayed generalization on modular arithmetic tasks. That is timely because it shifts the conversation from post-hoc interpretation toward causal architectural priors.
deferred.why_not_higher: The results live on toy algorithmic tasks rather than realistic models, datasets, or hardware settings, so the relevance to current AI practice is still speculative. The memory/storage tie is effectively absent despite Transformer terminology overlap.
downgrade_reasons: toy-task-only evidence
no direct memory/storage relevance
unclear impact on real-world model training
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate that the same topology choices materially improve generalization, efficiency, or training behavior in modern language-model workloads beyond modular arithmetic.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if grokking is less a mysterious phase transition and more a geometric byproduct of standard Transformer design choices?

deferred.title: Progressive Residual Warmup for Language Model Pretraining
deferred.abstract: Transformer architectures serve as the backbone for most modern Large Language Models, therefore their pretraining stability and convergence speed are of central concern. Motivated by the logical dependency of sequentially stacked layers, we propose Progressive Residual Warmup (ProRes) for language model pretraining. ProRes implements an "early layer learns first" philosophy by multiplying each layer's residual with a scalar that gradually warms up from 0 to 1, with deeper layers taking longer warmup steps. In this way, deeper layers wait for early layers to settle into a more stable regime before contributing to learning. We demonstrate the effectiveness of ProRes through pretraining experiments across various model scales, as well as normalization and initialization schemes. Comprehensive analysis shows that ProRes not only stabilizes pretraining but also introduces a unique optimization trajectory, leading to faster convergence, stronger generalization and better downstream performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/dandingsky/ProRes.
deferred.why_now: LLM pretraining is still expensive and brittle, so simple recipe changes that improve stability or convergence are worth tracking. ProRes is timely because it proposes a low-complexity training intervention that could matter if it holds up at larger scales.
deferred.why_not_higher: This reads more like a training-optimization tweak than a shift in how modern AI systems are built or served, and the abstract does not establish frontier-scale or production-scale validation. It also does not have a real memory/storage angle despite some embedding-level keyword overlap.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Broad appeal is narrower than top-cycle AI papers
Evidence for large-scale practical impact is not yet clear
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show repeatable gains on frontier-relevant pretraining runs with explicit compute savings and adoption in widely used open-source training stacks.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if LLM pretraining got more stable simply by letting early transformer layers learn first and only gradually waking up the deeper stack?

deferred.title: The Norm-Separation Delay Law of Grokking: A First-Principles Theory of Delayed Generalization
deferred.abstract: Grokking -- the sudden generalisation that appears long after a model has perfectly memorised its training data -- has been widely observed but lacks a quantitative theory explaining the length of the delay. We show that grokking is a norm-driven representational phase transition in regularised training dynamics, and establish the Norm-Separation Delay Law: $T_{\mathrm{grok}} - T_{\mathrm{mem}} = Θ(γ_{\mathrm{eff}}^{-1} \log(\|θ_{\mathrm{mem}}\|^2 / \|θ_{\mathrm{post}}\|^2))$, where $γ_{\mathrm{eff}}$ is the optimiser's effective contraction rate ($γ_{\mathrm{eff}} = ηλ$ for SGD, $γ_{\mathrm{eff}} \ge ηλ$ for AdamW). The upper bound follows from a discrete Lyapunov contraction argument; the matching lower bound from dynamical constraints of regularised first-order optimisation. Across 293 training runs spanning modular addition, modular multiplication, and sparse parity, we confirm three falsifiable predictions: inverse scaling with weight decay ($R^2 = 0.97$), inverse scaling with learning rate ($R^2 = 0.92$), and logarithmic dependence on the norm ratio (Pearson $r = 0.91$). A fourth finding reveals that grokking requires an optimiser capable of decoupling memorisation from contraction: SGD fails entirely at the same hyperparameters where AdamW reliably groks. These results reframe grokking not as a mysterious optimisation artefact but as a predictable consequence of norm separation between competing interpolating representations. We further derive a practical three-input algorithm that predicts grokking delay at memorisation time with 34.6% mean absolute error (bootstrap 95% CI [30.0%, 39.4%], $N=60$ seeds), enabling principled early stopping.
deferred.why_now: Grokking remains a live question in training dynamics, and this paper turns it into a falsifiable delay law with explicit optimizer-dependent predictions. That gives it real intellectual interest for people tracking how and when generalization emerges.
deferred.why_not_higher: The validation is on toy algorithmic tasks rather than realistic transformer training, large models, or production workloads. It also has no direct memory or storage mechanism and limited near-term impact on how modern AI systems are built or served.
downgrade_reasons: Toy workloads only
No direct memory/storage relevance
Unclear transfer to modern LLM training
Limited near-term practical impact
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate the law and the early-stopping predictor on realistic transformer pretraining or finetuning runs with modern-scale models.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if grokking is not mysterious at all, but a predictable countdown set by weight decay, learning rate, and the gap between memorizing and truly generalizing solutions?

deferred.title: NL2GDS: LLM-aided interface for Open Source Chip Design
deferred.abstract: The growing complexity of hardware design and the widening gap between high-level specifications and register-transfer level (RTL) implementation hinder rapid prototyping and system design. We introduce NL2GDS (Natural Language to Layout), a novel framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to translate natural language hardware descriptions into synthesizable RTL and complete GDSII layouts via the open-source OpenLane ASIC flow. NL2GDS employs a modular pipeline that captures informal design intent, generates HDL using multiple LLM engines and verifies them, and orchestrates automated synthesis and layout. Evaluations on ISCAS'85 and ISCAS'89 benchmark designs demonstrate up to 36% area reduction, 35% delay reduction, and 70% power savings compared to baseline designs, highlighting its potential to democratize ASIC design and accelerate hardware innovation.
deferred.why_now: LLMs are starting to move from chat and code into engineering toolchains, and a natural-language-to-GDS pipeline is a sharp demo of that ambition. The open-source flow also makes the democratized chip-design angle timely and easy to explain.
deferred.why_not_higher: The evidence is built on legacy ISCAS benchmarks rather than modern AI accelerators, memory subsystems, or production RTL blocks, so the reported gains may not transfer to hardware that matters for current AI. It also does not directly change how people train, serve, or evaluate frontier models today.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Validation is on old benchmark circuits rather than AI-relevant hardware
Relevance to modern AI system building is indirect
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing strong results on real AI accelerator components such as SRAM-heavy datapaths, NoCs, or cache/offload-related blocks would raise it substantially.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What happens when you ask an LLM to turn plain English into an actual chip layout, and how far is that from something AI hardware teams would trust?

deferred.title: Beyond Word Error Rate: Auditing the Diversity Tax in Speech Recognition through Dataset Cartography
deferred.abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems are predominantly evaluated using the Word Error Rate (WER). However, raw token-level metrics fail to capture semantic fidelity and routinely obscures the `diversity tax', the disproportionate burden on marginalized and atypical speaker due to systematic recognition failures. In this paper, we explore the limitations of relying solely on lexical counts by systematically evaluating a broader class of non-linear and semantic metrics. To enable rigorous model auditing, we introduce the sample difficulty index (SDI), a novel metric that quantifies how intrinsic demographic and acoustic factors drive model failure. By mapping SDI on data cartography, we demonstrate that metrics EmbER and SemDist expose hidden systemic biases and inter-model disagreements that WER ignores. Finally, our findings are the first steps towards a robust audit framework for prospective safety analysis, empowering developers to audit and mitigate ASR disparities prior to deployment.
deferred.why_now: Speech models are moving into more real products, and WER can hide whether they fail specific speaker groups while still looking acceptable on aggregate. A paper that pushes semantic and disparity-aware auditing is timely for teams evaluating voice AI responsibly.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is still an ASR-specific audit paper rather than a result that reshapes how most AI teams build, train, or serve models. It also has no meaningful memory/storage angle, so it does not contribute to the second editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: ASR-specific evaluation scope
No direct memory/storage contribution
Metric framework more than a systems or model advance
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show that these audit metrics materially change model selection or deployment decisions across multiple production ASR systems or transfer cleanly to multimodal and LLM evaluation.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the standard speech metric says your model is fine while it is still systematically failing the people it most needs to understand?

deferred.title: Knowledge Divergence and the Value of Debate for Scalable Oversight
deferred.abstract: AI safety via debate and reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF) are both proposed methods for scalable oversight of advanced AI systems, yet no formal framework relates them or characterizes when debate offers an advantage. We analyze this by parameterizing debate's value through the geometry of knowledge divergence between debating models. Using principal angles between models' representation subspaces, we prove that the debate advantage admits an exact closed form. When models share identical training corpora, debate reduces to RLAIF-like where a single-agent method recovers the same optimum. When models possess divergent knowledge, debate advantage scales with a phase transition from quadratic regime (debate offers negligible benefit) to linear regime (debate is essential). We classify three regimes of knowledge divergence (shared, one-sided, and compositional) and provide existence results showing that debate can achieve outcomes inaccessible to either model alone, alongside a negative result showing that sufficiently strong adversarial incentives cause coordination failure in the compositional regime, with a sharp threshold separating effective from ineffective debate. We offer the first formal connection between debate and RLAIF, a geometric foundation for understanding when adversarial oversight protocols are justified, and connection to the problem of eliciting latent knowledge across models with complementary information.
deferred.why_now: Scalable oversight, debate, and AI-generated feedback are timely as labs rely more on automated evaluation and RLAIF-style loops. This paper gives a clean formal account of when multi-agent debate should add value over single-agent oversight.
deferred.why_not_higher: The contribution is mostly theoretical, with no validation on realistic models, judges, or deployment settings. It also has no direct memory or storage mechanism, so it misses the podcast's second lens.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage connection
No realistic workload or model validation
Limited immediate impact on current build/train/serve practice
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Empirical results showing debate beats strong single-agent RLAIF baselines on real frontier-model oversight tasks would raise it materially.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: When do two imperfect AIs arguing uncover truth that one model alone would miss, and when do they just fail together?

deferred.title: Warm Starting State-Space Models with Automata Learning
deferred.abstract: We prove that Moore machines can be exactly realized as state-space models (SSMs), establishing a formal correspondence between symbolic automata and these continuous machine learning architectures. These Moore-SSMs preserve both the complete symbolic structure and input-output behavior of the original Moore machine, but operate in Euclidean space. With this correspondence, we compare the training of SSMs with both passive and active automata learning. In recovering automata from the SYNTCOMP benchmark, we show that SSMs require orders of magnitude more data than symbolic methods and fail to learn state structure. This suggests that symbolic structure provides a strong inductive bias for learning these systems. We leverage this insight to combine the strengths of both automata learning and SSMs in order to learn complex systems efficiently. We learn an adaptive arbitration policy on a suite of arbiters from SYNTCOMP and show that initializing SSMs with symbolically-learned approximations learn both faster and better. We see 2-5 times faster convergence compared to randomly initialized models and better overall model accuracies on test data. Our work lifts automata learning out of purely discrete spaces, enabling principled exploitation of symbolic structure in continuous domains for efficiently learning in complex settings.
deferred.why_now: State-space models are back in the conversation as alternatives or complements to transformers, and this paper offers a crisp symbolic-to-continuous initialization story. The main timely idea is that explicit automata structure can materially improve sample efficiency when training SSMs.
deferred.why_not_higher: The evidence is mostly on SYNTCOMP automata and arbiter benchmarks, not modern large-model training, inference, or serving workloads. The memory/storage angle is not direct, and the path to mainstream AI practice is still speculative.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Benchmarks are narrow and synthetic
Unclear impact on modern large-scale AI systems
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same warm-starting idea improving contemporary sequence models or real-world control/agent workloads with clear wins over standard initialization baselines.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the best way to train a continuous sequence model is to start from a symbolic machine that formal methods can already learn exactly?

deferred.title: Planning in 8 Tokens: A Compact Discrete Tokenizer for Latent World Model
deferred.abstract: World models provide a powerful framework for simulating environment dynamics conditioned on actions or instructions, enabling downstream tasks such as action planning or policy learning. Recent approaches leverage world models as learned simulators, but its application to decision-time planning remains computationally prohibitive for real-time control. A key bottleneck lies in latent representations: conventional tokenizers encode each observation into hundreds of tokens, making planning both slow and resource-intensive. To address this, we propose CompACT, a discrete tokenizer that compresses each observation into as few as 8 tokens, drastically reducing computational cost while preserving essential information for planning. An action-conditioned world model that occupies CompACT tokenizer achieves competitive planning performance with orders-of-magnitude faster planning, offering a practical step toward real-world deployment of world models.
deferred.why_now: World-model planning is still bottlenecked by bloated visual latents, and compressing each observation to 8 discrete tokens is a crisp attempt to make decision-time planning fast enough for control. That makes it timely within the current embodied-AI and efficient-planning discussion.
deferred.why_not_higher: The impact still looks concentrated in vision and robotics world-model benchmarks rather than mainstream AI training or serving practice, and the memory angle is token-efficiency adjacent rather than a first-class cache, offload, or bandwidth systems result. It also overlaps with a recent compact-latent world-model episode, so the freshness premium is limited.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow embodied-control scope
Memory connection is adjacent, not core
Benchmark-heavy evidence rather than real deployment
Recent overlap with compact world-model planning coverage
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same tokenizer improving multiple world-model families on harder real-world embodied workloads, with explicit latency and memory measurements on actual planning hardware.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if world-model planning stays slow not because the dynamics model is weak, but because we keep asking the planner to think in hundreds of visual tokens when eight might do?

deferred.title: Why Depth Matters in Parallelizable Sequence Models: A Lie Algebraic View
deferred.abstract: Scalable sequence models, such as Transformer variants and structured state-space models, often trade expressivity power for sequence-level parallelism, which enables efficient training. Here we examine the bounds on error and how error scales when models operate outside of their expressivity regimes using a Lie-algebraic control perspective. Our theory formulates a correspondence between the depth of a sequence model and the tower of Lie algebra extensions. Echoing recent theoretical studies, we characterize the Lie-algebraic class of constant-depth sequence models and their corresponding expressivity bounds. Furthermore, we analytically derive an approximation error bound and show that error diminishes exponentially as the depth increases, consistent with the strong empirical performance of these models. We validate our theoretical predictions using experiments on symbolic word and continuous-valued state-tracking problems.
deferred.why_now: Parallelizable sequence models are back in focus as people look beyond vanilla attention, and this paper gives a principled explanation for why shallow efficient models run into expressivity limits. It is timely as the field keeps trading off sequence-level parallelism against capability.
deferred.why_not_higher: The paper is mostly theory plus controlled symbolic and state-tracking experiments, not evidence on modern LLM-scale workloads, serving stacks, or hardware behavior. It also does not make a direct memory, cache, bandwidth, or storage contribution.
downgrade_reasons: theory-heavy with limited immediate builder impact
validation is on synthetic tasks rather than realistic large-model workloads
no direct memory or storage mechanism
unclear near-term effect on training or serving practice
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A clear link from the theory to concrete architecture choices or scaling results on modern Transformer or SSM workloads would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do efficient shallow sequence models hit a wall, and why can adding depth recover expressivity exponentially even when the model stays parallelizable?

deferred.title: Exploring the potential and limitations of Model Merging for Multi-Domain Adaptation in ASR
deferred.abstract: Model merging is a scalable alternative to multi-task training that combines the capabilities of multiple specialised models into a single model. This is particularly attractive for large speech foundation models, which are typically adapted through domain-specific fine-tuning, resulting in multiple customised checkpoints, for which repeating full fine-tuning when new data becomes available is computationally prohibitive. In this work, we study model merging for multi-domain ASR and benchmark 11 merging algorithms for 10 European Portuguese domains, evaluating in-domain accuracy, robustness under distribution shift, as well as English and multilingual performance. We further propose BoostedTSV-M, a new merging algorithm based on TSV-M that mitigates rank collapse via singular-value boosting and improves numerical stability. Overall, our approach outperforms full fine-tuning on European Portuguese while preserving out-of-distribution generalisation in a single model.
deferred.why_now: Model merging is a live question for teams managing many domain-tuned checkpoints, and this paper tests that idea at useful scale across 10 ASR domains. Its timely claim is that one merged model can beat repeated full fine-tuning while preserving some out-of-domain robustness.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is still a narrow ASR paper centered on European Portuguese, so the transfer to mainstream LLM practice is not yet proven. The memory/storage angle is weak because it does not address KV cache, bandwidth, offload, paging, or serving locality directly.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow ASR and language focus
Weak direct memory/storage relevance
Adjacent to ongoing model-merging coverage
Limited proof of transfer to broader foundation-model workflows
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A result on widely used multilingual speech or LLM checkpoints with clear deployment and checkpoint-management wins would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you replace a zoo of domain-specific speech checkpoints with one merged model that actually beats re-fine-tuning from scratch?

deferred.title: Layer by layer, module by module: Choose both for optimal OOD probing of ViT
deferred.abstract: Recent studies have observed that intermediate layers of foundation models often yield more discriminative representations than the final layer. While initially attributed to autoregressive pretraining, this phenomenon has also been identified in models trained via supervised and discriminative self-supervised objectives. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive study to analyze the behavior of intermediate layers in pretrained vision transformers. Through extensive linear probing experiments across a diverse set of image classification benchmarks, we find that distribution shift between pretraining and downstream data is the primary cause of performance degradation in deeper layers. Furthermore, we perform a fine-grained analysis at the module level. Our findings reveal that standard probing of transformer block outputs is suboptimal; instead, probing the activation within the feedforward network yields the best performance under significant distribution shift, whereas the normalized output of the multi-head self-attention module is optimal when the shift is weak.
deferred.why_now: As more teams reuse frozen foundation encoders, this paper makes a timely evaluation point: the final ViT layer can be the wrong place to probe under distribution shift. Its module-level result is a useful warning that quick linear-probe readouts may understate transferable performance.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is mainly a probing and benchmarking paper, not a result that changes how most people build, train, or serve AI systems. It also has no direct memory/storage mechanism and its practical impact is still concentrated in vision representation evaluation.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily an evaluation/probing study rather than a systems advance
Vision-centric scope limits near-term cross-model relevance
Episode would risk feeling like a niche benchmark discussion
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A clear demonstration that the same module-selection rule improves transfer for LLMs, multimodal encoders, or production retrieval and classification pipelines would raise priority.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: If the best OOD features in a ViT are not at the top, have we been probing the wrong layer and even the wrong module all along?

deferred.title: Score-Guided Proximal Projection: A Unified Geometric Framework for Rectified Flow Editing
deferred.abstract: Rectified Flow (RF) models achieve state-of-the-art generation quality, yet controlling them for precise tasks -- such as semantic editing or blind image recovery -- remains a challenge. Current approaches bifurcate into inversion-based guidance, which suffers from "geometric locking" by rigidly adhering to the source trajectory, and posterior sampling approximations (e.g., DPS), which are computationally expensive and unstable. In this work, we propose Score-Guided Proximal Projection (SGPP), a unified framework that bridges the gap between deterministic optimization and stochastic sampling. We reformulate the recovery task as a proximal optimization problem, defining an energy landscape that balances fidelity to the input with realism from the pre-trained score field. We theoretically prove that this objective induces a normal contraction property, geometrically guaranteeing that out-of-distribution inputs are snapped onto the data manifold, and it effectively reaches the posterior mode constrained to the manifold. Crucially, we demonstrate that SGPP generalizes state-of-the-art editing methods: RF-inversion is effectively a limiting case of our framework. By relaxing the proximal variance, SGPP enables "soft guidance," offering a continuous, training-free trade-off between strict identity preservation and generative freedom.
deferred.why_now: Rectified-flow control is an active subtopic in generative modeling, and a training-free method that unifies inversion-style editing with posterior-style guidance is a real algorithmic contribution. The geometric framing could shape how RF editing and inverse problems are discussed in the near term.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is still mostly a generative-vision editing paper, not a paper that changes how mainstream AI systems are built, trained, or served. The memory/storage link is absent, and the abstract does not show clear deployment-scale or hardware-grounded evidence.
downgrade_reasons: narrow generative-vision editing focus
no direct memory or storage mechanism
limited systems relevance outside rectified-flow workflows
evidence appears benchmark-centric from the abstract
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate clear wins on widely used large-scale rectified-flow models with strong practical impact on real editing or recovery pipelines.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a single geometric rule let rectified-flow models edit images more freely without getting trapped by inversion?

deferred.title: Distributed Partial Information Puzzles: Examining Common Ground Construction Under Epistemic Asymmetry
deferred.abstract: Establishing common ground, a shared set of beliefs and mutually recognized facts, is fundamental to collaboration, yet remains a challenge for current AI systems, especially in multimodal, multiparty settings, where the collaborators bring different information to the table. We introduce the Distributed Partial Information Puzzle (DPIP), a collaborative construction task that elicits rich multimodal communication under epistemic asymmetry. We present a multimodal dataset of these interactions, annotated and temporally aligned across speech, gesture, and action modalities to support reasoning over propositional content and belief dynamics. We then evaluate two paradigms for modeling common ground (CG): (1) state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs), prompted to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, and (2) an axiomatic pipeline grounded in Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) that incrementally performs the same task. Results on the annotated DPIP data indicate that it poses a challenge to modern LLMs' abilities to track both task progression and belief state.
deferred.why_now: Agentic and multimodal AI systems are pushing into collaborative settings where tracking shared belief state actually matters, and this paper gives a concrete stress test for that capability. It is timely as an evaluation paper for people worried that current LLMs still fake coordination rather than maintain common ground.
deferred.why_not_higher: It does not directly change how frontier systems are trained, served, or optimized, and the memory/storage connection is absent despite superficial overlap with 'memory' language. The contribution is mainly a task, dataset, and benchmark showing a capability gap rather than a method with clear operational impact.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily a benchmark/dataset paper
Limited immediate systems impact
Task is narrower than broad foundation-model practice
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the benchmark drives new training methods or evaluation standards for multimodal agents in real deployed workflows would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do modern multimodal models still lose the plot in simple collaborative tasks that humans solve by constantly updating shared belief?

deferred.title: S-Path-RAG: Semantic-Aware Shortest-Path Retrieval Augmented Generation for Multi-Hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering
deferred.abstract: We present S-Path-RAG, a semantic-aware shortest-path Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework designed to improve multi-hop question answering over large knowledge graphs. S-Path-RAG departs from one-shot, text-heavy retrieval by enumerating bounded-length, semantically weighted candidate paths using a hybrid weighted $k$-shortest, beam, and constrained random-walk strategy, learning a differentiable path scorer together with a contrastive path encoder and lightweight verifier, and injecting a compact soft mixture of selected path latents into a language model via cross-attention. The system runs inside an iterative Neural-Socratic Graph Dialogue loop in which concise diagnostic messages produced by the language model are mapped to targeted graph edits or seed expansions, enabling adaptive retrieval when the model expresses uncertainty. This combination yields a retrieval mechanism that is both token-efficient and topology-aware while preserving interpretable path-level traces for diagnostics and intervention. We validate S-Path-RAG on standard multi-hop KGQA benchmarks and through ablations and diagnostic analyses. The results demonstrate consistent improvements in answer accuracy, evidence coverage, and end-to-end efficiency compared to strong graph- and LLM-based baselines. We further analyze trade-offs between semantic weighting, verifier filtering, and iterative updates, and report practical recommendations for deployment under constrained compute and token budgets.
deferred.why_now: Teams are still looking for grounded RAG methods that use fewer tokens and expose interpretable evidence, and this paper pushes that idea into structured graph retrieval rather than brute-force text stuffing. The adaptive retrieval loop is timely because reliability under tight context budgets is still a live problem.
