deferred.why_not_higher: The contribution is mostly confined to KGQA, which is narrower than the mainstream LLM stack most listeners care about, and it is not yet clear that the path machinery changes how general RAG systems are built. Despite the efficiency framing, it does not make a direct memory, cache, or storage contribution.
downgrade_reasons: KGQA benchmark niche
No direct memory/storage mechanism
Transfer to general-purpose RAG is unproven
Benchmark-heavy evidence rather than clear deployment evidence
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the method improves production-scale structured RAG or agent workloads on messy real-world graphs, with strong latency and cost comparisons against standard retrieval baselines, would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if RAG handled multi-hop questions by tracing explicit knowledge-graph paths instead of cramming ever more text into the context window?

deferred.title: LTLGuard: Formalizing LTL Specifications with Compact Language Models and Lightweight Symbolic Reasoning
deferred.abstract: Translating informal requirements into formal specifications is challenging due to the ambiguity and variability of natural language (NL). This challenge is particularly pronounced when relying on compact (small and medium) language models, which may lack robust knowledge of temporal logic and thus struggle to produce syntactically valid and consistent formal specifications. In this work, we focus on enabling resource-efficient open-weight models (4B--14B parameters) to generate correct linear temporal logic (LTL) specifications from informal requirements. We present LTLGuard, a modular toolchain that combines constrained generation with formal consistency checking to generate conflict-free LTL specifications from informal input. Our method integrates the generative capabilities of model languages with lightweight automated reasoning tools to iteratively refine candidate specifications, understand the origin of the conflicts and thus help in eliminating inconsistencies. We demonstrate the usability and the effectiveness of our approach and perform quantitative evaluation of the resulting framework.
deferred.why_now: There is real current interest in making smaller open models dependable, and this paper shows a concrete verifier-in-the-loop pattern rather than relying on prompt-only fixes. It also taps into the broader push for structured, checkable LLM outputs in engineering workflows.
deferred.why_not_higher: The target problem is still fairly niche, and the path from NL-to-LTL translation to mainstream AI training, serving, or evaluation is indirect. It also has no meaningful memory/storage angle, which removes one of the show's core lenses.
downgrade_reasons: formal methods niche
no direct memory/storage mechanism
indirect impact on mainstream AI systems
real-world workload breadth unclear
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the same constrained-generation plus symbolic-checking loop materially improves production LLM coding, agent, or safety workflows beyond LTL specification generation.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a 4B model become reliable at formal reasoning once you stop trusting it blindly and force it to work with a symbolic checker?

deferred.title: Interpretable Perception and Reasoning for Audiovisual Geolocation
deferred.abstract: While recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have improved image-based localization, precise global geolocation remains a formidable challenge due to the inherent ambiguity of visual landscapes and the largely untapped potential of auditory cues. In this paper, we introduce Audiovisual Geolocation, a framework designed to resolve geographic ambiguity through interpretable perception and reasoning. We present AVG, a high-quality global-scale video benchmark for geolocation, comprising 20,000 curated clips across 1,000 distinct locations. To address the complexity of audiovisual geolocation, we propose a three-stage framework: (1) a Perception stage that utilizes a mixture-autoregressive sparse autoencoder to decompose noisy audio into semantically grounded "acoustic atoms"; (2) a Multimodal Reasoning stage that employs an MLLM finetuned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to synthesize these atoms with visual features; and (3) a Precision Prediction stage using Riemannian Flow Matching on the $S^2$ manifold. Our experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms unimodal baselines. These results entail that interpretable perception of the soundscape provides a critical, orthogonal signal that, when coupled with multimodal reasoning, enables high-precision global localization.
deferred.why_now: It taps into a timely multimodal question: whether audio can resolve failures of vision-only geolocation and whether interpretable sound primitives help reasoning. The new global-scale benchmark also makes this a cleaner evaluation target than prior anecdotal demos.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is still a narrow geolocation paper, and the method stack looks tailored to that task more than like a reusable recipe for mainstream AI systems. It does not clearly change how people build, train, serve, or evaluate frontier models outside this niche, and the memory/storage connection is absent.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow geolocation application
Weak transfer to mainstream AI workflows
No direct memory/storage mechanism
Mostly benchmark-plus-task-specific method
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the interpretable audiovisual reasoning pipeline transfers cleanly to broader multimodal grounding or evaluation tasks used by frontier MLLMs.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a model hear where it is when the visuals are ambiguous, or is audiovisual geolocation still a clever niche benchmark rather than a step toward more grounded multimodal reasoning?

deferred.title: Thermodynamic Response Functions in Singular Bayesian Models
deferred.abstract: Singular statistical models-including mixtures, matrix factorization, and neural networks-violate regular asymptotics due to parameter non-identifiability and degenerate Fisher geometry. Although singular learning theory characterizes marginal likelihood behavior through invariants such as the real log canonical threshold and singular fluctuation, these quantities remain difficult to interpret operationally. At the same time, widely used criteria such as WAIC and WBIC appear disconnected from underlying singular geometry. We show that posterior tempering induces a one-parameter deformation of the posterior distribution whose associated observables generate a hierarchy of thermodynamic response functions. A universal covariance identity links derivatives of tempered expectations to posterior fluctuations, placing WAIC, WBIC, and singular fluctuation within a unified response framework. Within this framework, classical quantities from singular learning theory acquire natural thermodynamic interpretations: RLCT governs the leading free-energy slope, singular fluctuation corresponds to curvature of the tempered free energy, and WAIC measures predictive fluctuation. We formalize an observable algebra that quotients out non-identifiable directions, allowing structurally meaningful order parameters to be constructed in singular models. Across canonical singular examples-including symmetric Gaussian mixtures, reduced-rank regression, and overparameterized neural networks-we empirically demonstrate phase-transition-like behavior under tempering. Order parameters collapse, susceptibilities peak, and complexity measures align with structural reorganization in posterior geometry. Our results suggest that thermodynamic response theory provides a natural organizing framework for interpreting complexity, predictive variability, and structural reorganization in singular Bayesian learning.
deferred.why_now: As overparameterized models keep breaking classical statistical intuitions, a framework that gives WAIC, WBIC, RLCT, and singular fluctuation a common interpretation is timely for theory-minded evaluation work. It is most relevant as a conceptual lens for singular Bayesian learning rather than a near-term systems result.
deferred.why_not_higher: The paper does not show a clear path to changing how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated at production scale, and the evidence appears centered on canonical singular examples rather than realistic frontier workloads or hardware. Its memory/storage relevance is effectively absent.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Limited validation on large modern workloads
Unclear near-term operational impact for most AI practitioners
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing these response functions as useful diagnostics or model-selection tools for large contemporary models would raise priority.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the strange geometry of overparameterized models could be read like thermodynamics, turning WAIC, WBIC, and singular learning theory into a phase diagram for Bayesian deep learning?

deferred.title: Wiki-R1: Incentivizing Multimodal Reasoning for Knowledge-based VQA via Data and Sampling Curriculum
deferred.abstract: Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA) requires models to answer questions about an image by integrating external knowledge, posing significant challenges due to noisy retrieval and the structured, encyclopedic nature of the knowledge base. These characteristics create a distributional gap from pretrained multimodal large language models (MLLMs), making effective reasoning and domain adaptation difficult in the post-training stage. In this work, we propose \textit{Wiki-R1}, a data-generation-based curriculum reinforcement learning framework that systematically incentivizes reasoning in MLLMs for KB-VQA. Wiki-R1 constructs a sequence of training distributions aligned with the model's evolving capability, bridging the gap from pretraining to the KB-VQA target distribution. We introduce \textit{controllable curriculum data generation}, which manipulates the retriever to produce samples at desired difficulty levels, and a \textit{curriculum sampling strategy} that selects informative samples likely to yield non-zero advantages during RL updates. Sample difficulty is estimated using observed rewards and propagated to unobserved samples to guide learning. Experiments on two KB-VQA benchmarks, Encyclopedic VQA and InfoSeek, demonstrate that Wiki-R1 achieves new state-of-the-art results, improving accuracy from 35.5\% to 37.1\% on Encyclopedic VQA and from 40.1\% to 44.1\% on InfoSeek. The project page is available at https://artanic30.github.io/project_pages/WikiR1/.
deferred.why_now: Multimodal reasoning and post-training with RL are active themes, and this paper targets a real weakness: getting MLLMs to use external knowledge instead of relying on pretraining alone. The curriculum idea is timely because retrieval noise and distribution shift are still major blockers for knowledge-grounded VLMs.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is still a narrow KB-VQA paper with benchmark-specific wins, and the gains are modest enough that it does not obviously change mainstream multimodal system design. The memory/storage lens is essentially absent.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow KB-VQA task focus
No direct memory/storage mechanism
Benchmark-driven result more than systems shift
Unclear transfer to general-purpose multimodal assistants
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the curriculum method improves broader retrieval-grounded multimodal agents or real production-style assistants would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can curriculum RL actually teach multimodal models to reason with Wikipedia, or is Wiki-R1 mainly a cleaner path to a small benchmark bump?

deferred.title: Structured Multidimensional Representation Learning for Large Language Models
deferred.abstract: Transformer architectures achieve state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of pattern recognition and natural language processing tasks, but their scaling is accompanied by substantial parameter growth and redundancy in the embedding dimension. In this work, we introduce a structured spectral factorization of the embedding space based on the L-product for third-order tensors. By reshaping token representations into spectral tensor slices and performing attention and feed-forward operations in the transform domain, we obtain a Tensor Transformer architecture that decomposes the encoder into p independent spectral sub-transformers while preserving standard Transformer semantics. We prove that the proposed L-Transformer is spectrally equivalent to p parallel Transformers operating on reduceddimensional embeddings, which yields approximately 1/p reduction (up to lower-order terms such as biases and normalization parameters) in encoder parameters under fixed total embedding size. When instantiated with a real-valued Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), the method remains fully differentiable and compatible with existing training pipelines. Beyond compression, the spectral decomposition introduces an inductive bias over embedding frequencies, enabling slice-dependent frequency scaling that improves generalization. Experiments on IMDB and AG~News show that the proposed model can substantially reduce encoder parameters (up to 75\% for p=4) while maintaining competitive accuracy. On IMDB, the tensorized encoder matches or improves upon the standard baseline under compression, whereas on AG~News at moderate width we observe a small accuracy decrease in exchange for a 4 times encoder reduction; at BERT-base width (d=768), performance returns to parity.
deferred.why_now: Parameter-efficient transformer design still matters as model footprints keep growing, and this paper offers a structured spectral factorization story instead of another ad hoc compression trick. The idea is timely because it targets embedding redundancy while staying differentiable and compatible with standard training pipelines.
deferred.why_not_higher: The validation is too far from modern LLM practice: encoder-only results on IMDB and AG News do not show that this changes how current models are built or served. The memory angle is also indirect, since this is parameter compression rather than KV-cache, bandwidth, paging, tiering, or offload work.
downgrade_reasons: Small, dated benchmark mix
No causal LLM or long-context evidence
Memory/storage relevance is indirect
No hardware, latency, or bandwidth measurements
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show parity on modern decoder or long-context models with real training and serving memory, bandwidth, and latency measurements.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you split a transformer's embedding space into frequency slices and keep the behavior while cutting most of the encoder parameters?

deferred.title: Preserving Continuous Symmetry in Discrete Spaces: Geometric-Aware Quantization for SO(3)-Equivariant GNNs
deferred.abstract: Equivariant Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are essential for physically consistent molecular simulations but suffer from high computational costs and memory bottlenecks, especially with high-order representations. While low-bit quantization offers a solution, applying it naively to rotation-sensitive features destroys the SO(3)-equivariant structure, leading to significant errors and violations of conservation laws. To address this issue, in this work, we propose a Geometric-Aware Quantization (GAQ) framework that compresses and accelerates equivariant models while rigorously preserving continuous symmetry in discrete spaces. Our approach introduces three key contributions: (1) a Magnitude-Direction Decoupled Quantization (MDDQ) scheme that separates invariant lengths from equivariant orientations to maintain geometric fidelity; (2) a symmetry-aware training strategy that treats scalar and vector features with distinct quantization schedules; and (3) a robust attention normalization mechanism to stabilize gradients in low-bit regimes. Experiments on the rMD17 benchmark demonstrate that our W4A8 models match the accuracy of FP32 baselines (9.31 meV vs. 23.20 meV) while reducing Local Equivariance Error (LEE) by over 30x compared to naive quantization. On consumer hardware, GAQ achieves 2.39x inference speedup and 4x memory reduction, enabling stable, energy-conserving molecular dynamics simulations for nanosecond timescales.
deferred.why_now: Low-bit efficiency work is expanding beyond LLMs, and this paper makes the timely point that naive quantization can break the geometric structure scientific AI models depend on. It gives a concrete path to getting speed and memory wins on consumer hardware without destroying physical consistency.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is still a niche result centered on SO(3)-equivariant molecular GNNs rather than a method likely to shift mainstream AI practice in the next 30-90 days. The memory angle is real but adjacent, since the gain comes from quantizing a specialized model family rather than introducing a new cache, offload, paging, or serving mechanism.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow to SO(3)-equivariant molecular simulation models
Memory benefit comes from quantization rather than a first-class storage or cache mechanism
Evaluation is solid but concentrated on rMD17 and limited deployment settings
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the symmetry-preserving quantization recipe transfers to broader 3D, robotics, or multimodal architectures on more varied real-world workloads would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you compress a physics-aware neural simulator down to 4-bit memory without breaking the symmetry that keeps its world consistent?

deferred.title: Random Dot Product Graphs as Dynamical Systems: Limitations and Opportunities
deferred.abstract: Can we learn the differential equations governing the evolution of a temporal network? We investigate this within Random Dot Product Graphs (RDPGs), where each network snapshot is generated from latent positions evolving under unknown dynamics. We identify three fundamental obstructions: gauge freedom from rotational ambiguity in latent positions, realizability constraints from the manifold structure of the probability matrix, and trajectory recovery artifacts from spectral embedding.   We develop a geometric framework based on principal fiber bundles that formalizes these obstructions. We characterize invisible dynamics as exactly the skew-symmetric generators, and show the realizable tangent space has dimension $nd - d(d-1)/2$. An holonomy dichotomy emerges: polynomial dynamics have commuting generators, stationary eigenvectors, and trivial holonomy, making gauge alignment purely statistical; Laplacian dynamics satisfy a non-commutativity criterion producing nontrivial holonomy, with curvature weighted by $1/(λ_ι+ λ_γ)$ linking gauge sensitivity to the spectral gap. In $d=2$ this yields full restricted holonomy $\mathrm{SO}(2)$; for $d \ge 3$ generic full $\mathrm{SO}(d)$ remains conjectural. Cram'er--Rao lower bounds reveal that the same spectral gap controlling curvature and injectivity simultaneously controls Fisher information, so geometric and statistical difficulty are inextricable.   We prove an identifiability principle: symmetric dynamics cannot absorb skew-symmetric gauge contamination, so dynamics structure can resolve gauge ambiguity. We demonstrate this constructively with anchor-based alignment and a UDE pipeline recovering vector fields from noisy graph sequences. Yet finite-sample interactions between noise, gauge, and dynamics expressiveness remain beyond the asymptotic theory. We frame this gap as an open challenge.
deferred.why_now: Dynamic graph modeling and neural differential-equation tooling are increasingly used to infer hidden mechanisms from relational time series, and this paper makes the identifiability limits explicit. Its gauge-freedom result is a useful warning against over-interpreting recovered dynamics from graph snapshots.
deferred.why_not_higher: The link to current mainstream AI practice is weak: this is mostly geometry and statistics theory for temporal random graph models, not something that changes how modern foundation models are built, trained, served, or evaluated. The constructive pipeline is interesting, but it is not validated on realistic large-scale AI workloads or systems.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Limited relevance to modern AI system design
Empirical support is proof-of-concept rather than realistic AI workloads
Broad audience appeal is low outside temporal graph theory
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show that these identifiability and alignment results improve a real large-scale AI pipeline, such as dynamic graph learning or structured memory modules, with clear gains over standard recovery methods.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the dynamics you recover from a changing network are partly an illusion created by hidden rotational symmetry, and the math can tell you exactly which motions are forever invisible?

deferred.title: Scalable Digital Compute-in-Memory Ising Machines for Robustness Verification of Binary Neural Networks
deferred.abstract: Verification of binary neural network (BNN) robustness is NP-hard, as it can be formulated as a combinatorial search for an adversarial perturbation that induces misclassification. Exact verification methods therefore scale poorly with problem dimension, motivating the use of hardware-accelerated heuristics and unconventional computing platforms, such as Ising solvers, that can efficiently explore complex energy landscapes and discover high-quality solutions. In this work, we reformulate BNN robustness verification as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem and solve it using a digital compute-in-memory (DCIM) SRAM-based Ising machine. Instead of requiring globally optimal solutions, we exploit imperfect solutions produced by the DCIM Ising machine to extract adversarial perturbations and thereby demonstrate the non-robustness of the BNN. The proposed architecture stores quantized QUBO coefficients in approximately 9.1~Mb of SRAM and performs annealing in memory via voltage-controlled pseudo-read dynamics, enabling iterative updates with minimal data movement. Experimental projections indicate that the proposed approach achieves a $178\times$ acceleration in convergence rate and a $1538\times$ improvement in power efficiency relative to conventional CPU-based implementations.
deferred.why_now: Robustness verification is still a hard evaluation problem, and this paper ties it to a concrete compute-in-memory architecture that explicitly attacks data movement. That makes it timely for listeners tracking unconventional AI hardware and efficient evaluation methods.
deferred.why_not_higher: The target is binary neural networks, not the models driving current AI deployment, and the headline gains are projection-heavy rather than demonstrated on broadly realistic mainstream AI workloads. The memory angle is real at the hardware level but still adjacent to the podcast's core KV-cache, offload, paging, and tiering themes.
downgrade_reasons: Binary neural network verification is a narrow target
Weak connection to current LLM training or serving practice
Claims rely heavily on projected speed and power gains
Memory relevance is compute-in-memory adjacent, not core AI memory systems work
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show measured results on contemporary deployed models or quantized transformer workloads, with silicon-backed evidence that compute-in-memory verification materially changes real AI evaluation cost or safety workflows.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an SRAM-based compute-in-memory Ising machine turn adversarial robustness search from a painful software bottleneck into an in-memory hardware problem, or is this still a niche trick for binary networks?

out_of_scope.title: Scaling Real-Time Traffic Analytics on Edge-Cloud Fabrics for City-Scale Camera Networks
out_of_scope.abstract: Real-time city-scale traffic analytics requires processing 100s-1000s of CCTV streams under strict latency, bandwidth, and compute limits. We present a scalable AI-driven Intelligent Transportation System (AIITS) designed to address multi-dimensional scaling on an edge-cloud fabric. Our platform transforms live multi-camera video feeds into a dynamic traffic graph through a DNN inferencing pipeline, complemented by real-time nowcasting and short-horizon forecasting using Spatio-Temporal GNNs. Using a testbed to validate in a Bengaluru neighborhood, we ingest 100+ RTSP feeds from Raspberry Pis, while Jetson Orin edge accelerators perform high-throughput detection and tracking, producing lightweight flow summaries for cloud-based GNN inference. A capacity-aware scheduler orchestrates load-balancing across heterogeneous devices to sustain real-time performance as stream counts increase. To ensure continuous adaptation, we integrate SAM3 foundation-model assisted labeling and Continuous Federated Learning to update DNN detectors on the edge. Experiments show stable ingestion up to 2000 FPS on Jetson Orins, low-latency aggregation, and accurate and scalable ST-GNN forecasts for up to 1000 streams. A planned live demonstration will scale the full pipeline to 1000 streams, showcasing practical, cross-fabric scalability.
out_of_scope.why_now: Edge AI deployments are increasingly constrained by bandwidth, latency, and heterogeneous hardware, and this paper shows a concrete city-scale architecture running on real edge devices. It is timely as more inference stacks are being split across edge and cloud rather than centralized end to end.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The contribution is mostly a vertical traffic-analytics integration and scaling story, not a broadly reusable AI systems primitive. Its memory/storage relevance is adjacent through bandwidth-aware placement and compact summaries, not a first-class cache, paging, offload, or tiering result.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow traffic-analytics application
Memory/storage mechanism is indirect
Limited relevance to a broad AI audience
More integration benchmark than general AI method
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A general-purpose edge serving abstraction with explicit bandwidth and memory tradeoff measurements that transfer beyond traffic analytics would raise priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What does it take to turn a thousand city cameras into a real-time AI traffic graph without saturating the network or overwhelming the edge?

out_of_scope.title: Oral to Web: Digitizing 'Zero Resource'Languages of Bangladesh
out_of_scope.abstract: We present the Multilingual Cloud Corpus, the first national-scale, parallel, multimodal linguistic dataset of Bangladesh's ethnic and indigenous languages. Despite being home to approximately 40 minority languages spanning four language families, Bangladesh has lacked a systematic, cross-family digital corpus for these predominantly oral, computationally "zero resource" varieties, 14 of which are classified as endangered. Our corpus comprises 85792 structured textual entries, each containing a Bengali stimulus text, an English translation, and an IPA transcription, together with approximately 107 hours of transcribed audio recordings, covering 42 language varieties from the Tibeto-Burman, Indo-European, Austro-Asiatic, and Dravidian families, plus two genetically unclassified languages. The data were collected through systematic fieldwork over 90 days across nine districts of Bangladesh, involving 16 data collectors, 77 speakers, and 43 validators, following a predefined elicitation template of 2224 unique items organized at three levels of linguistic granularity: isolated lexical items (475 words across 22 semantic domains), grammatical constructions (887 sentences across 21 categories including verbal conjugation paradigms), and directed speech (862 prompts across 46 conversational scenarios). Post-field processing included IPA transcription by 10 linguists with independent adjudication by 6 reviewers. The complete dataset is publicly accessible through the Multilingual Cloud platform (multiling.cloud), providing searchable access to annotated audio and textual data for all documented varieties. We describe the corpus design, fieldwork methodology, dataset structure, and per-language coverage, and discuss implications for endangered language documentation, low-resource NLP, and digital preservation in linguistically diverse developing countries.
out_of_scope.why_now: The paper matters because current multilingual AI still has severe blind spots around endangered and low-resource languages, and this corpus creates real digital infrastructure where almost none existed. It is timely as a data and preservation effort, especially for speech and language coverage beyond major languages.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: It does not materially change how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated for the broader field, and the contribution is mainly a regional corpus release rather than a general method or systems advance. The memory/storage lens is not relevant here.
downgrade_reasons: Broad-AI urgency is limited
No direct systems or model-building impact
Memory/storage connection absent
Primarily a regional resource paper
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show strong downstream gains on multilingual ASR, translation, or LLM evaluation that transfer beyond these specific languages and make the corpus consequential for mainstream model development.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What does it actually take to bring dozens of mostly oral languages into the AI era, and why does that hard data work matter before any model breakthrough arrives?

out_of_scope.title: Reclaiming Lost Text Layers for Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
out_of_scope.abstract: Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (SF-CDFSL) focuses on fine-tuning with limited training data from target domains (e.g., medical or satellite images), where CLIP has recently shown promising results due to its generalizability to downstream tasks. Current works indicate CLIP's text encoder is more suitable for cross-domain tasks, however, we find that \textbf{removing certain middle layers of the text encoder can effectively improve performance in SF-CDFSL}, which we call the Lost Layers. In this paper, we delve into this phenomenon for a deeper understanding. We discover that instead of being harmful for the SF-CDFSL task, the information in these layers is actually beneficial, but visual gaps prevent this useful information from being fully utilized, making these layers seem redundant. Based on this understanding, unlike current works that simply remove these layers, we propose a method to teachs the model to \textbf{re-utilize} information in these lost layers at both the layer and encoder levels, guiding the re-learning of the visual branch under domain shifts. Our approach effectively addresses the issue of underutilized information in the text encoder. Extensive experiments across various settings, backbones (CLIP, SigLip, PE-Core), and tasks (4 CDFSL datasets and 10 Meta-dataset datasets) demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Code is available at https://github.com/zhenyuZ-HUST/CVPR26-VtT.
out_of_scope.why_now: The paper identifies a non-obvious CLIP adaptation effect: middle text layers that look redundant under domain shift may still contain useful signal. That is mildly interesting for vision-language transfer work, especially in low-data specialized domains.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The setup is still a narrow SF-CDFSL benchmark track rather than a result that changes mainstream model building, serving, or evaluation. It also has no direct memory or storage mechanism, so it misses the podcast's stronger editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Source-free cross-domain few-shot learning is a niche task setting
Benchmarks are broad academically but not clearly deployment-facing
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the same layer-reuse idea improves mainstream multimodal adaptation workflows or real production transfer settings beyond SF-CDFSL benchmarks.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the CLIP text layers researchers keep deleting are not dead weight, but stranded knowledge that domain shift prevents the visual branch from using?

out_of_scope.title: PersianPunc: A Large-Scale Dataset and BERT-Based Approach for Persian Punctuation Restoration
out_of_scope.abstract: Punctuation restoration is essential for improving the readability and downstream utility of automatic speech recognition (ASR) outputs, yet remains underexplored for Persian despite its importance. We introduce PersianPunc, a large-scale, high-quality dataset of 17 million samples for Persian punctuation restoration, constructed through systematic aggregation and filtering of existing textual resources. We formulate punctuation restoration as a token-level sequence labeling task and fine-tune ParsBERT to achieve strong performance. Through comparative evaluation, we demonstrate that while large language models can perform punctuation restoration, they suffer from critical limitations: over-correction tendencies that introduce undesired edits beyond punctuation insertion (particularly problematic for speech-to-text pipelines) and substantially higher computational requirements. Our lightweight BERT-based approach achieves a macro-averaged F1 score of 91.33% on our test set while maintaining efficiency suitable for real-time applications. We make our dataset (https://huggingface.co/datasets/MohammadJRanjbar/persian-punctuation-restoration) and model (https://huggingface.co/MohammadJRanjbar/parsbert-persian-punctuation) publicly available to facilitate future research in Persian NLP and provide a scalable framework applicable to other morphologically rich, low-resource languages.
out_of_scope.why_now: It releases a very large Persian punctuation dataset and makes a practical case that a small task-specific model can outperform LLM-style approaches for ASR post-processing. That is timely for multilingual NLP teams looking for cheaper, more controllable inference paths.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is still a narrow punctuation-restoration paper tied to a specific language and task, not a paper that changes how the broader AI field builds or serves modern systems. The memory/storage connection is effectively absent beyond generic efficiency claims.
downgrade_reasons: narrow task paper
limited relevance to mainstream AI workflows
no direct memory or storage mechanism
transfer beyond Persian is asserted more than demonstrated
episode would lean benchmark-heavy
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger cross-lingual transfer story plus deployment results on real ASR pipelines with clear latency, cost, and error-control comparisons would make it more episode-worthy.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why does a compact Persian BERT beat larger LLM-style approaches at punctuation repair, and what does that say about when specialized models still win?

out_of_scope.title: SarcasmMiner: A Dual-Track Post-Training Framework for Robust Audio-Visual Sarcasm Reasoning
out_of_scope.abstract: Multimodal sarcasm detection requires resolving pragmatic incongruity across textual, acoustic, and visual cues through cross-modal reasoning. To enable robust sarcasm reasoning with foundation models, we propose SarcasmMiner, a reinforcement learning based post-training framework that resists hallucination in multimodal reasoning. We reformulate sarcasm detection as structured reasoning and adopt a dual-track distillation strategy: high-quality teacher trajectories initialize the student model, while the full set of trajectories trains a generative reward model (GenRM) to evaluate reasoning quality. The student is optimized with group relative policy optimization (GRPO) using decoupled rewards for accuracy and reasoning quality. On MUStARD++, SarcasmMiner increases F1 from 59.83% (zero-shot), 68.23% (supervised finetuning) to 70.22%. These findings suggest that reasoning-aware reward modeling enhances both performance and multimodal grounding.
out_of_scope.why_now: Reasoning-aware post-training and multimodal grounding are active themes, and this paper applies RL with a reasoning-quality reward to a genuinely tricky audio-visual language task. It is timely as an example of trying to reduce hallucinated multimodal explanations rather than just boosting task accuracy.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The evidence is still centered on a narrow sarcasm benchmark with modest gains, not a broadly adopted multimodal workload. The method may be interesting, but the paper does not show that it changes how most teams train or evaluate modern AI systems.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow task focus
No direct memory/storage relevance
Single-benchmark evidence
Unclear transfer to broader multimodal reasoning
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same post-training recipe improving several mainstream multimodal reasoning tasks or foundation models with clear cross-dataset transfer and stronger ablations.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a reward model teach multimodal AI to catch sarcasm without inventing reasons it never truly grounded in the video, audio, and text?

out_of_scope.title: A Behaviour-Aware Federated Forecasting Framework for Distributed Stand-Alone Wind Turbines
out_of_scope.abstract: Accurate short-term wind power forecasting is essential for grid dispatch and market operations, yet centralising turbine data raises privacy, cost, and heterogeneity concerns. We propose a two-stage federated learning framework that first clusters turbines by long-term behavioural statistics using Double Roulette Selection (DRS) initialisation with recursive Auto-split refinement, and then trains cluster-specific LSTM models via FedAvg. Experiments on 400 stand-alone turbines in Denmark show that DRS-auto discovers behaviourally coherent groups and achieves competitive forecasting accuracy while preserving data locality. Behaviour-aware grouping consistently outperforms geographic partitioning and matches strong k-means++ baselines, suggesting a practical privacy-friendly solution for heterogeneous distributed turbine fleets.
out_of_scope.why_now: Privacy-preserving learning on distributed industrial assets is a real operational need, and behavior-aware grouping is a sensible way to handle non-IID turbine fleets. That makes the paper timely for edge forecasting and energy operations practitioners.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow wind-power forecasting paper built around fairly conventional ingredients like clustering, LSTM models, and FedAvg. It does not clearly change how a broad AI audience builds, trains, serves, or evaluates modern AI systems, and the memory/storage link is absent.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow application domain
No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited transfer to frontier AI systems
Method stack is conventional rather than field-shifting
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger systems story showing transfer to broader edge AI workloads or concrete memory/bandwidth/locality gains across heterogeneous distributed models would raise priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can federated learning make scattered wind turbines forecast better without centralizing their data, or is this just a solid niche engineering result?

out_of_scope.title: PACE: A Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine for 9-1-1 Call-taker Training
out_of_scope.abstract: 9-1-1 call-taking training requires mastery of over a thousand interdependent skills, covering diverse incident types and protocol-specific nuances. A nationwide labor shortage is already straining training capacity, but effective instruction still demands that trainers tailor objectives to each trainee's evolving competencies. This personalization burden is one that current practice cannot scale. Partnering with Metro Nashville Department of Emergency Communications (MNDEC), we propose PACE (Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine), a co-pilot system that augments trainer decision-making by (1) maintaining probabilistic beliefs over trainee skill states, (2) modeling individual learning and forgetting dynamics, and (3) recommending training scenarios that balance acquisition of new competencies with retention of existing ones. PACE propagates evidence over a structured skill graph to accelerate diagnostic coverage and applies contextual bandits to select scenarios that target gaps the trainee is prepared to address. Empirical results show that PACE achieves 19.50% faster time-to-competence and 10.95% higher terminal mastery compared to state-of-the-art frameworks. Co-pilot studies with practicing training officers further demonstrate a 95.45% alignment rate between PACE's and experts' pedagogical judgments on real-world cases. Under estimation, PACE cuts turnaround time to merely 34 seconds from 11.58 minutes, up to 95.08% reduction.
out_of_scope.why_now: Staffing shortages in emergency communications make better training tooling timely, and this paper reports deployment-grounded gains with a real 9-1-1 partner. It is a useful example of AI decision support in a high-stakes public-service workflow.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: It does not change how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated, and the memory/storage link is absent despite the language about forgetting. The contribution is also tightly tied to one specialized training domain, with limited evidence of broader transfer.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow domain application
No direct systems or model-building impact
Memory/storage relevance is absent
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the same curriculum engine generalizes across multiple high-stakes training domains or transfers to training and evaluation pipelines for AI agents themselves.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an AI co-pilot meaningfully speed up 9-1-1 call-taker training without replacing the expert judgment that emergency response depends on?

out_of_scope.title: An Exploration-Analysis-Disambiguation Reasoning Framework for Word Sense Disambiguation with Low-Parameter LLMs
out_of_scope.abstract: Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) remains a key challenge in Natural Language Processing (NLP), especially when dealing with rare or domain-specific senses that are often misinterpreted. While modern high-parameter Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4-Turbo have shown state-of-the-art WSD performance, their computational and energy demands limit scalability. This study investigates whether low-parameter LLMs (<4B parameters) can achieve comparable results through fine-tuning strategies that emphasize reasoning-driven sense identification. Using the FEWS dataset augmented with semi-automated, rationale-rich annotations, we fine-tune eight small-scale open-source LLMs (e.g. Gemma and Qwen). Our results reveal that Chain-of-Thought (CoT)-based reasoning combined with neighbour-word analysis achieves performance comparable to GPT-4-Turbo in zero-shot settings. Importantly, Gemma-3-4B and Qwen-3-4B models consistently outperform all medium-parameter baselines and state-of-the-art models on FEWS, with robust generalization to unseen senses. Furthermore, evaluation on the unseen "Fool Me If You Can'' dataset confirms strong cross-domain adaptability without task-specific fine-tuning. This work demonstrates that with carefully crafted reasoning-centric fine-tuning, low-parameter LLMs can deliver accurate WSD while substantially reducing computational and energy demands.
out_of_scope.why_now: It sits in the current small-model efficiency conversation by arguing that careful reasoning-centric fine-tuning can let sub-4B models match or beat larger baselines on WSD. The cross-domain check on unseen senses gives it some credibility beyond a single benchmark split.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is still a narrow task paper about word sense disambiguation, not a result that is likely to change mainstream LLM building or serving practice. The memory/storage link is absent, and the systems implications are indirect rather than central.
downgrade_reasons: narrow NLP task
no direct memory/storage mechanism
limited impact on mainstream AI system design
episode would center on benchmark gains more than a broader shift
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the same reasoning-centric recipe reliably improves other hard semantic or retrieval-heavy LLM tasks at similar cost would make it more editorially relevant.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a 4B model beat bigger LLMs at tricky word-sense reasoning, and does that signal a real efficiency lesson or just a well-tuned niche benchmark?

out_of_scope.title: GALACTIC: Global and Local Agnostic Counterfactuals for Time-series Clustering
out_of_scope.abstract: Time-series clustering is a fundamental tool for pattern discovery, yet existing explainability methods, primarily based on feature attribution or metadata, fail to identify the transitions that move an instance across cluster boundaries. While Counterfactual Explanations (CEs) identify the minimal temporal perturbations required to alter the prediction of a model, they have been mostly confined to supervised settings. This paper introduces GALACTIC, the first unified framework to bridge local and global counterfactual explainability for unsupervised time-series clustering. At instance level (local), GALACTIC generates perturbations via a cluster-aware optimization objective that respects the target and underlying cluster assignments. At cluster level (global), to mitigate cognitive load and enhance interpretability, we formulate a representative CE selection problem. We propose a Minimum Description Length (MDL) objective to extract a non-redundant summary of global explanations that characterize the transitions between clusters. We prove that our MDL objective is supermodular, which allows the corresponding MDL reduction to be framed as a monotone submodular set function. This enables an efficient greedy selection algorithm with provable $(1-1/e)$ approximation guarantees. Extensive experimental evaluation on the UCR Archive demonstrates that GALACTIC produces significantly sparser local CEs and more concise global summaries than state-of-the-art baselines adapted for our problem, offering the first unified approach for interpreting clustered time-series through counterfactuals.
out_of_scope.why_now: Counterfactual explanations for unsupervised time-series clustering are a legitimate technical gap, and the local-plus-global framing is methodologically clean. The paper is timely mainly within XAI for time-series, not within the main current AI conversation.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This does not materially change how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated at scale, and the memory/storage connection is absent. The evidence is benchmark-driven on UCR-style datasets rather than realistic large-model or production settings.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow time-series clustering focus
No direct memory or storage mechanism
Limited relevance to modern foundation-model workflows
Benchmark-centered validation rather than real deployment settings
Weak episode potential beyond a specialized XAI audience
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A clear demonstration that the method transfers to foundation-model-era sequence systems or production monitoring workflows would raise it.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you explain not just why a time series landed in one cluster, but what minimal temporal change would push it into another?

out_of_scope.title: Latent Wasserstein Adversarial Imitation Learning
out_of_scope.abstract: Imitation Learning (IL) enables agents to mimic expert behavior by learning from demonstrations. However, traditional IL methods require large amounts of medium-to-high-quality demonstrations as well as actions of expert demonstrations, both of which are often unavailable. To reduce this need, we propose Latent Wasserstein Adversarial Imitation Learning (LWAIL), a novel adversarial imitation learning framework that focuses on state-only distribution matching. It benefits from the Wasserstein distance computed in a dynamics-aware latent space. This dynamics-aware latent space differs from prior work and is obtained via a pre-training stage, where we train the Intention Conditioned Value Function (ICVF) to capture a dynamics-aware structure of the state space using a small set of randomly generated state-only data. We show that this enhances the policy's understanding of state transitions, enabling the learning process to use only one or a few state-only expert episodes to achieve expert-level performance. Through experiments on multiple MuJoCo environments, we demonstrate that our method outperforms prior Wasserstein-based IL methods and prior adversarial IL methods, achieving better results across various tasks.
out_of_scope.why_now: Learning from one or a few state-only demonstrations is a real constraint in imitation learning, so the paper is timely for RL and robotics researchers working on low-supervision control. The dynamics-aware latent matching idea is the main reason to pay attention now.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is still a niche imitation-learning paper with evidence centered on MuJoCo benchmarks rather than modern foundation-model training or serving. It has no direct memory/storage mechanism and limited relevance to the podcast's two core lenses.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage contribution
Narrow imitation-learning audience
Evidence limited to simulator benchmarks
Weak path to current large-model practice
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A convincing link to large-scale agent training or real robotics settings, especially showing impact beyond MuJoCo and a clearer connection to how modern AI systems are built or evaluated, would raise it.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can imitation learning reach expert performance from just a few state-only demos by matching behavior in a dynamics-aware latent space?

out_of_scope.title: Behavior-dLDS: A decomposed linear dynamical systems model for neural activity partially constrained by behavior
out_of_scope.abstract: Brain-wide recordings of large-scale networks of neurons now provide an unprecedented view into how the brain drives behavior. However, brain activity contains both information directly related to behavior as well as the potential for many internal computations. Moreover, observable behavior is executed not only by the brain, but also by the spinal cord and peripheral nervous system. Behavior is a coarse-grained product of neural activity, and we thus take the view that it can be best represented by lower-dimensional latent neural dynamics. Capturing this indirect relationship while disambiguating behavior-generating networks from internal computations running in parallel requires new modeling approaches that can embody the parallel and distributed nature of large-scale neural populations. We thus present behavior-decomposed linear dynamical systems (b-dLDS) to disentangle simultaneously recorded subsystems and identify how the latent neural subsystems relate to behavior. We demonstrate the ability of b-dLDS to decouple behavioral vs. internal computations on controlled, simulated data, showing improvements over a state-of-the-art model that uses behavior to supervise all dynamics based on behavior. We also demonstrate b-dLDS's interpretability benefits on a task-driven RNN dataset featuring a nonlinear relationship between behavior and activations. We then show that b-dLDS can further scale up to tens of thousands of neurons by applying our model to a large-scale recording of a zebrafish hindbrain during the complex positional homeostasis behavior, wherein b-dLDS highlights asymmetry in behavior-related dynamic connectivity networks.
out_of_scope.why_now: It is an interesting neuroscience-plus-ML methods paper about separating behavior-linked dynamics from parallel internal computation, which gives it some interpretability appeal. That said, the relevance is mostly to neural data analysis rather than to the current AI build/train/serve stack.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: It does not change how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated, and the memory/storage connection is absent rather than direct. The experiments are solid for its domain, but the transfer path from zebrafish neural recordings to mainstream AI practice is speculative.
downgrade_reasons: Neuroscience analysis paper rather than modern AI systems work
No direct memory, cache, storage, bandwidth, or offload mechanism
Limited relevance to current foundation-model training or inference
Transfer path to broad AI practice is unclear
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A concrete demonstration that the same decomposition improves interpretability, controllability, or state-space modeling in contemporary AI systems would raise it materially.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the trick to separating action from hidden computation in neural networks first shows up in a zebrafish brain?

out_of_scope.title: NERdME: a Named Entity Recognition Dataset for Indexing Research Artifacts in Code Repositories
out_of_scope.abstract: Existing scholarly information extraction (SIE) datasets focus on scientific papers and overlook implementation-level details in code repositories. README files describe datasets, source code, and other implementation-level artifacts, however, their free-form Markdown offers little semantic structure, making automatic information extraction difficult. To address this gap, NERdME is introduced: 200 manually annotated README files with over 10,000 labeled spans and 10 entity types. Baseline results using large language models and fine-tuned transformers show clear differences between paperlevel and implementation-level entities, indicating the value of extending SIE benchmarks with entity types available in README files. A downstream entity-linking experiment was conducted to demonstrate that entities derived from READMEs can support artifact discovery and metadata integration.
out_of_scope.why_now: AI research increasingly ships through code repositories, and README files often contain the artifact metadata that papers omit. Structuring that layer is timely for reproducibility and discovery tooling.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow README-focused NER benchmark, not a paper that changes how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated. The memory/storage connection is absent, and the episode would mostly be about dataset creation and baseline extraction results.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Narrow information-extraction benchmark
Limited immediate impact on model building, training, serving, or evaluation
Modest dataset scale for broad editorial significance
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that README-level entity extraction materially improves large-scale discovery, reuse, and evaluation of models, datasets, and code across major AI repositories.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the real bottleneck in reusing AI research is not the paper, but the messy README where the actual artifacts are hidden?

out_of_scope.title: VietJobs: A Vietnamese Job Advertisement Dataset
out_of_scope.abstract: VietJobs is the first large-scale, publicly available corpus of Vietnamese job advertisements, comprising 48,092 postings and over 15 million words collected from all 34 provinces and municipalities across Vietnam. The dataset provides extensive linguistic and structured information, including job titles, categories, salaries, skills, and employment conditions, covering 16 occupational domains and multiple employment types (full-time, part-time, and internship). Designed to support research in natural language processing and labour market analytics, VietJobs captures substantial linguistic, regional, and socio-economic diversity. We benchmark several generative large language models (LLMs) on two core tasks: job category classification and salary estimation. Instruction-tuned models such as Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-SEA-LION-v3-8B-IT demonstrate notable gains under few-shot and fine-tuned settings, while highlighting challenges in multilingual and Vietnamese-specific modelling for structured labour market prediction. VietJobs establishes a new benchmark for Vietnamese NLP and offers a valuable foundation for future research on recruitment language, socio-economic representation, and AI-driven labour market analysis. All code and resources are available at: https://github.com/VinNLP/VietJobs.
out_of_scope.why_now: It is a timely multilingual resource for Vietnamese NLP and labour-market analysis, and the benchmark usefully probes how current instruction-tuned LLMs handle structured recruitment tasks. That matters for a niche audience tracking non-English data coverage and applied evaluation gaps.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is primarily a domain dataset and benchmark paper, not a paper that changes how the field builds, trains, serves, or evaluates modern AI systems at large. The memory/storage connection is absent, and the transfer beyond Vietnamese job ads is limited.
downgrade_reasons: narrow application dataset
no direct memory/storage mechanism
limited broad-AI audience pull
benchmark more than method
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that the dataset or evaluation findings expose general multilingual LLM failure modes that transfer across languages, hiring markets, or broader structured-information extraction tasks would raise it.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What can a 48,000-post Vietnamese jobs dataset teach us about where modern LLMs still break on real-world multilingual labour-market language?

out_of_scope.title: SecureRAG-RTL: A Retrieval-Augmented, Multi-Agent, Zero-Shot LLM-Driven Framework for Hardware Vulnerability Detection
out_of_scope.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in natural language processing tasks, yet their application in hardware security verification remains limited due to scarcity of publicly available hardware description language (HDL) datasets. This knowledge gap constrains LLM performance in detecting vulnerabilities within HDL designs. To address this challenge, we propose SecureRAG-RTL, a novel Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based approach that significantly enhances LLM-based security verification of hardware designs. Our approach integrates domain-specific retrieval with generative reasoning, enabling models to overcome inherent limitations in hardware security expertise. We establish baseline vulnerability detection rates using prompt-only methods and then demonstrate that SecureRAG-RTL achieves substantial improvements across diverse LLM architectures, regardless of size. On average, our method increases detection accuracy by about 30%, highlighting its effectiveness in bridging domain knowledge gaps. For evaluation, we curated and annotated a benchmark dataset of 14 HDL designs containing real-world security vulnerabilities, which we will release publicly to support future research. These findings underscore the potential of RAG-driven augmentation to enable scalable, efficient, and accurate hardware security verification workflows.
out_of_scope.why_now: It addresses a live question around whether RAG can make general LLMs useful in expert engineering domains with little public training data, here hardware security verification. The planned public release of an HDL vulnerability benchmark gives this niche a reusable artifact.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow hardware-security application paper, not a result that changes how most people build, serve, or evaluate modern AI systems. The evidence is also limited for a framework claim: a 14-design benchmark and no stronger real-world workflow or expert-baseline story.
downgrade_reasons: narrow domain application
no direct memory/storage mechanism
small benchmark
limited transfer shown beyond HDL security review
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A much larger public benchmark plus evidence that the method transfers into real verification toolchains or other security/code domains would raise its priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can retrieval turn a generic LLM into a useful hardware security auditor, or is SecureRAG-RTL mostly a small-benchmark proof of concept?

out_of_scope.title: An interpretable prototype parts-based neural network for medical tabular data
out_of_scope.abstract: The ability to interpret machine learning model decisions is critical in such domains as healthcare, where trust in model predictions is as important as their accuracy. Inspired by the development of prototype parts-based deep neural networks in computer vision, we propose a new model for tabular data, specifically tailored to medical records, that requires discretization of diagnostic result norms. Unlike the original vision models that rely on the spatial structure, our method employs trainable patching over features describing a patient, to learn meaningful prototypical parts from structured data. These parts are represented as binary or discretized feature subsets. This allows the model to express prototypes in human-readable terms, enabling alignment with clinical language and case-based reasoning. Our proposed neural network is inherently interpretable and offers interpretable concept-based predictions by comparing the patient's description to learned prototypes in the latent space of the network. In experiments, we demonstrate that the model achieves classification performance competitive to widely used baseline models on medical benchmark datasets, while also offering transparency, bridging the gap between predictive performance and interpretability in clinical decision support.
out_of_scope.why_now: Interpretability in healthcare remains important, and prototype-style explanations for tabular medical data are a legitimate clinical ML theme. The paper offers a readable case-based framing rather than a black-box classifier.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is still a narrow medical tabular application paper, not something that changes mainstream AI system design or evaluation. The method also has no direct memory/storage relevance and limited transfer to current foundation-model practice.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow medical tabular scope
No direct effect on modern AI system building or serving
Memory/storage connection absent
Evidence appears limited to benchmark-style medical datasets
Interpretability angle is useful but not broadly catalytic
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger case that the method transfers beyond medical tabular benchmarks into broadly used AI workflows or real clinical deployment would raise priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a prototype-based neural net explain medical decisions in human clinical terms without sacrificing too much accuracy?

out_of_scope.title: Koopman Regularized Deep Speech Disentanglement for Speaker Verification
out_of_scope.abstract: Human speech contains both linguistic content and speaker dependent characteristics making speaker verification a key technology in identity critical applications. Modern deep learning speaker verification systems aim to learn speaker representations that are invariant to semantic content and nuisance factors such as ambient noise. However, many existing approaches depend on labelled data, textual supervision or large pretrained models as feature extractors, limiting scalability and practical deployment, raising sustainability concerns. We propose Deep Koopman Speech Disentanglement Autoencoder (DKSD-AE), a structured autoencoder that combines a novel multi-step Koopman operator learning module with instance normalization to disentangle speaker and content dynamics. Quantitative experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that DKSD-AE achieves improved or competitive speaker verification performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines while maintaining high content EER, confirming effective disentanglement. These results are obtained with substantially fewer parameters and without textual supervision. Moreover, performance remains stable under increased evaluation scale, highlighting representation robustness and generalization. Our findings suggest that Koopman-based temporal modelling, when combined with instance normalization, provides an efficient and principled solution for speaker-focused representation learning.
out_of_scope.why_now: Efficient speaker verification still matters for identity-sensitive and on-device speech products, and this paper argues for smaller text-free disentanglement instead of larger supervised pipelines. The parameter-efficiency angle is timely, but the impact is mostly within speech biometrics.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow speaker-verification paper, not something that clearly changes mainstream AI training, serving, or evaluation. The Koopman framing is interesting, but the memory/storage connection is absent and the evidence is benchmark-focused rather than systems-deployment focused.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow speech application
No direct memory/storage mechanism
Limited relevance to mainstream AI system design
Benchmark gains without clear deployment impact
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the disentanglement method transfers to broader speech or multimodal representation learning and demonstrate clear gains in real deployment settings.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a Koopman-style autoencoder separate who is speaking from what is being said without transcripts or heavyweight pretrained models?

out_of_scope.title: FreeTxt-Vi: A Benchmarked Vietnamese-English Toolkit for Segmentation, Sentiment, and Summarisation
out_of_scope.abstract: FreeTxt-Vi is a free and open source web based toolkit for creating and analysing bilingual Vietnamese English text collections. Positioned at the intersection of corpus linguistics and natural language processing NLP it enables users to build explore and interpret free text data without requiring programming expertise. The system combines corpus analysis features such as concordancing keyword analysis word relation exploration and interactive visualisation with transformer based NLP components for sentiment analysis and summarisation. A key contribution of this work is the design of a unified bilingual NLP pipeline that integrates a hybrid VnCoreNLP and Byte Pair Encoding BPE segmentation strategy a fine tuned TabularisAI sentiment classifier and a fine tuned Qwen2.5 model for abstractive summarisation. Unlike existing text analysis platforms FreeTxt Vi is evaluated as a set of language processing components. We conduct a three part evaluation covering segmentation sentiment analysis and summarisation and show that our approach achieves competitive or superior performance compared to widely used baselines in both Vietnamese and English. By reducing technical barriers to multilingual text analysis FreeTxt Vi supports reproducible research and promotes the development of language resources for Vietnamese a widely spoken but underrepresented language in NLP. The toolkit is applicable to domains including education digital humanities cultural heritage and the social sciences where qualitative text data are common but often difficult to process at scale.
out_of_scope.why_now: Accessible, benchmarked tooling for Vietnamese-English NLP matters because low-resource and bilingual text analysis still lags behind mainstream English-first workflows. The paper is timely as more researchers want usable summarisation and sentiment pipelines without building custom infrastructure.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This looks more like a solid integrated toolkit and benchmark paper than a result that changes mainstream AI practice. It has little connection to memory/storage systems, and its methods do not obviously generalize into a broader shift in how modern models are built or served.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage relevance is absent
Narrow bilingual application/toolkit focus
Limited impact on core model or systems practice
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that its pipeline design or evaluation approach transfers cleanly to multiple underrepresented languages or changes multilingual NLP practice beyond this specific toolkit would raise it.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a practical Vietnamese-English NLP toolkit expose the real bottleneck in multilingual AI: model quality, or the lack of usable tools for everyone outside big labs?

out_of_scope.title: NCTB-QA: A Large-Scale Bangla Educational Question Answering Dataset and Benchmarking Performance
out_of_scope.abstract: Reading comprehension systems for low-resource languages face significant challenges in handling unanswerable questions. These systems tend to produce unreliable responses when correct answers are absent from context. To solve this problem, we introduce NCTB-QA, a large-scale Bangla question answering dataset comprising 87,805 question-answer pairs extracted from 50 textbooks published by Bangladesh's National Curriculum and Textbook Board. Unlike existing Bangla datasets, NCTB-QA maintains a balanced distribution of answerable (57.25%) and unanswerable (42.75%) questions. NCTB-QA also includes adversarially designed instances containing plausible distractors. We benchmark three transformer-based models (BERT, RoBERTa, ELECTRA) and demonstrate substantial improvements through fine-tuning. BERT achieves 313% relative improvement in F1 score (0.150 to 0.620). Semantic answer quality measured by BERTScore also increases significantly across all models. Our results establish NCTB-QA as a challenging benchmark for Bangla educational question answering. This study demonstrates that domain-specific fine-tuning is critical for robust performance in low-resource settings.
out_of_scope.why_now: Low-resource language QA and reliable abstention on unanswerable questions are still weak spots, so a large balanced Bangla benchmark is useful evaluation infrastructure. It also pushes against the field's English-centric benchmark bias.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is mostly a narrow dataset-and-benchmark paper, not a result that changes how mainstream AI systems are built, served, or evaluated. The experiments rely on older transformer baselines rather than modern multilingual LLM or tutoring-system setups.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow educational QA benchmark
No direct memory/storage relevance
Limited transfer beyond Bangla textbook QA
Baselines are dated relative to current LLM practice
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger result on modern multilingual LLMs showing broadly transferable gains in abstention, calibration, or tutoring reliability would raise priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the real test for educational AI is not answering correctly, but knowing when the textbook does not contain the answer at all?

out_of_scope.title: Interpretable Motion Artificat Detection in structural Brain MRI
out_of_scope.abstract: Automated quality assessment of structural brain MRI is an important prerequisite for reliable neuroimaging analysis, but yet remains challenging due to motion artifacts and poor generalization across acquisition sites. Existing approaches based on image quality metrics (IQMs) or deep learning either requires extensive preprocessing, which incurs high computational cost, or poor generalization to unseen data. In this work, we propose a lightweight and interpretable framework for detecting motion related artifacts in T1 weighted brain MRI by extending the Discriminative Histogram of Gradient Magnitude (DHoGM) to a three dimensional space. The proposed method integrates complementary slice-level (2D) and volume-level (3D) DHoGM features through a parallel decision strategy, capturing both localized and global motion-induced degradation. Volumetric analysis is performed using overlapping 3D cuboids to achieve comprehensive spatial coverage while maintaining computational efficiency. A simple threshold-based classifier and a low parameter multilayer perceptron are used, which results in a model with only 209 trainable parameters. Our method was evaluated on the MR-ART and ABIDE datasets under both seen-site and unseen-site conditions. Experimental results demonstrate strong performance, achieving up to 94.34\% accuracy the in domain evaluation and 89\% accuracy on unseen sites, while almost completely avoiding false acceptance of poor-quality scans. Ablation studies confirms the complementary benefits of combining 2D and 3D features. Overall, the proposed approach offers an effective, efficient, and robust solution for automated MRI quality check, with strong potential for integration into large scale clinical and research workflows.
out_of_scope.why_now: Reliable automated MRI quality control matters as clinical and research imaging pipelines scale across sites, and this paper offers a lightweight, interpretable approach with decent unseen-site results. The low-parameter design is practically appealing for deployment in constrained healthcare workflows.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow medical imaging QA paper, not a paper that changes how the broader AI field builds, trains, serves, or evaluates modern AI systems. The memory/storage connection is absent, and the core method does not obviously transfer to current foundation-model bottlenecks.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow medical imaging quality-control task
No direct memory, cache, storage, or systems mechanism
Limited relevance to mainstream AI model building or serving
Transferability beyond MRI artifact detection is unclear
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the interpretable feature pipeline generalizes into broader AI data-quality or model-evaluation workflows beyond MRI would raise its priority.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a 209-parameter interpretable model catch bad brain MRI scans well enough to beat heavier quality-control pipelines across hospitals?

out_of_scope.title: Kraus Constrained Sequence Learning For Quantum Trajectories from Continuous Measurement
out_of_scope.abstract: Real-time reconstruction of conditional quantum states from continuous measurement records is a fundamental requirement for quantum feedback control, yet standard stochastic master equation (SME) solvers require exact model specification, known system parameters, and are sensitive to parameter mismatch. While neural sequence models can fit these stochastic dynamics, the unconstrained predictors can violate physicality such as positivity or trace constraints, leading to unstable rollouts and unphysical estimates. We propose a Kraus-structured output layer that converts the hidden representation of a generic sequence backbone into a completely positive trace preserving (CPTP) quantum operation, yielding physically valid state updates by construction. We instantiate this layer across diverse backbones, RNN, GRU, LSTM, TCN, ESN and Mamba; including Neural ODE as a comparative baseline, on stochastic trajectories characterized by parameter drift. Our evaluation reveals distinct trade-offs between gating mechanisms, linear recurrence, and global attention. Across all models, Kraus-LSTM achieves the strongest results, improving state estimation quality by 7% over its unconstrained counterpart while guaranteeing physically valid predictions in non-stationary regimes.
out_of_scope.why_now: Constraint-by-construction sequence modeling is a live theme, and this paper applies it cleanly to non-stationary quantum state estimation. The timely idea is enforcing valid state updates by design rather than hoping an unconstrained model learns them.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The contribution is anchored in quantum feedback control, not in how modern AI systems are built, trained, served, or evaluated. It also has no direct memory, cache, bandwidth, or storage systems story.
downgrade_reasons: Quantum control application is too niche for a broad AI audience
No direct memory or storage mechanism
Benchmarks are domain-specific rather than mainstream AI workloads
Transfer path to modern AI systems is weak from the abstract alone
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same constraint-layer idea improving robustness or long-rollout stability in mainstream AI sequence modeling or systems-control tasks.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the real lesson from quantum trajectory learning is that sequence models become more reliable when validity is built into the output layer itself?

out_of_scope.title: Longitudinal Lesion Inpainting in Brain MRI via 3D Region Aware Diffusion
out_of_scope.abstract: Accurate longitudinal analysis of brain MRI is often hindered by evolving lesions, which bias automated neuroimaging pipelines. While deep generative models have shown promise in inpainting these lesions, most existing methods operate cross-sectionally or lack 3D anatomical continuity. We present a novel pseudo-3D longitudinal inpainting framework based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM). Our approach utilizes multi-channel conditioning to incorporate longitudinal context from distinct visits (t_1, t_2) and extends Region-Aware Diffusion (RAD) to the medical domain, focusing the generative process on pathological regions without altering surrounding healthy tissue. We evaluated our model against state-of-the-art baselines on longitudinal brain MRI from 93 patients. Our model significantly outperforms the leading baseline (FastSurfer-LIT) in terms of perceptual fidelity, reducing the Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) distance from 0.07 to 0.03 while effectively eliminating inter-slice discontinuities. Furthermore, our model demonstrates high longitudinal stability with a Temporal Fidelity Index of 1.024, closely approaching the ideal value of 1.0 and substantially narrowing the gap compared to LIT's TFI of 1.22. Notably, the RAD mechanism provides a substantial gain in efficiency; our framework achieves an average processing time of 2.53 min per volume, representing approximately 10x speedup over the 24.30 min required by LIT. By leveraging longitudinal priors and region-specific denoising, our framework provides a highly reliable and efficient preprocessing step for the study of progressive neurodegenerative diseases. A derivative dataset consisting of 93 pre-processed scans used for testing will be available upon request after acceptance. Code will be released upon acceptance.
out_of_scope.why_now: It is a solid medical-imaging paper that adapts diffusion to longitudinal 3D lesion inpainting with better temporal consistency and a meaningful runtime gain over a strong baseline. That makes it timely for neuroimaging workflows studying disease progression.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The contribution is tightly scoped to brain MRI lesion preprocessing and does not clearly change how the broader AI field builds, trains, serves, or evaluates modern systems.
